title: Memphis, Tennessee

infobox.value: [John Overton](/wiki/John_Overton_(judge) "John Overton (judge)"), [James Winchester](/wiki/James_Winchester_(general) "James Winchester (general)"), and [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson")

[77th](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population") in North America [28th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States [2nd](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Tennessee "List of municipalities in Tennessee") in Tennessee

([/ˈmɛmfɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [*MEM-fis*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a city in [Shelby County, Tennessee](/wiki/Shelby_County,_Tennessee "Shelby County, Tennessee"), United States, and its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). Situated along the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), it had a population of 633,104 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), making it the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Tennessee "List of municipalities in Tennessee") in Tennessee, the fifth-most populous in the [Southeast](/wiki/Southeastern_United_States "Southeastern United States"), and the [28th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the US. Memphis is the largest city proper on the Mississippi River and anchors the [Memphis area](/wiki/Memphis_metropolitan_area "Memphis metropolitan area") that includes parts of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") and [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"), the [45th-most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. with 1.34 million residents.

**Memphis** ([/ˈmɛmfɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [*MEM-fis*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a city in [Shelby County, Tennessee](/wiki/Shelby_County,_Tennessee "Shelby County, Tennessee"), United States, and its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). Situated along the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), it had a population of 633,104 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), making it the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Tennessee "List of municipalities in Tennessee") in Tennessee, the fifth-most populous in the [Southeast](/wiki/Southeastern_United_States "Southeastern United States"), and the [28th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the US. Memphis is the largest city proper on the Mississippi River and anchors the [Memphis area](/wiki/Memphis_metropolitan_area "Memphis metropolitan area") that includes parts of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") and [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"), the [45th-most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. with 1.34 million residents.

European exploration of the area began with Spanish conquistador [Hernando de Soto](/wiki/Hernando_de_Soto "Hernando de Soto") in 1541. Located on the high [Chickasaw Bluffs](/wiki/Chickasaw_Bluff "Chickasaw Bluff"), the site offered natural protection from Mississippi River flooding and became a contested location in the colonial era. Modern Memphis was founded in 1819 by [John Overton](/wiki/John_Overton_(judge) "John Overton (judge)"), [James Winchester](/wiki/James_Winchester_(general) "James Winchester (general)"), and [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson"). The city thrived due to its river traffic and cotton-based economy, becoming one of the largest cities in the [Antebellum South](/wiki/Antebellum_South "Antebellum South"). After the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), it remained a key hub for the cotton and hardwood industries. Memphis is also notable for its role in the [American Civil Rights Movement](/wiki/American_Civil_Rights_Movement "American Civil Rights Movement"); Dr. [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") was [assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.") there in 1968, and the city is now home to the [National Civil Rights Museum](/wiki/National_Civil_Rights_Museum "National Civil Rights Museum"), a [Smithsonian](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") affiliate.

Memphis is one of the nation's leading commercial centers in transportation and [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The largest employer is [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx"), which maintains its global air hub at [Memphis International Airport](/wiki/Memphis_International_Airport "Memphis International Airport"), one of the world's busiest cargo airports.[[11]](#cite_note-2023Final-11) The [Port of Memphis](/wiki/Port_of_Memphis "Port of Memphis") also hosts the fifth-busiest inland water port in the U.S.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Memphis is also known for its [music scene](/wiki/Music_of_Tennessee "Music of Tennessee"), with [Beale Street](/wiki/Beale_Street "Beale Street") central to the development of [Memphis blues](/wiki/Memphis_blues "Memphis blues") and a broader legacy that includes [soul](/wiki/Memphis_soul "Memphis soul"), rock and roll, and [hip-hop](/wiki/Memphis_rap "Memphis rap"). Cultural landmarks include [Graceland](/wiki/Graceland "Graceland"), [Sun Studio](/wiki/Sun_Studio "Sun Studio"), the [Memphis Pyramid](/wiki/Memphis_Pyramid "Memphis Pyramid"), and [Stax Museum of American Soul Music](/wiki/Stax_Museum_of_American_Soul_Music "Stax Museum of American Soul Music"). The city is also famed for its [Memphis-style barbecue](/wiki/Memphis-style_barbecue "Memphis-style barbecue") and hosts the annual [World Championship Barbecue Cooking Contest](/wiki/Memphis_in_May#World_Championship_Barbecue_Cooking_Contest_(WCBCC) "Memphis in May"). It is home to the [Memphis Grizzlies](/wiki/Memphis_Grizzlies "Memphis Grizzlies") of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") and several colleges and universities, including the [University of Memphis](/wiki/University_of_Memphis "University of Memphis"), [LeMoyne–Owen College](/wiki/LeMoyne%E2%80%93Owen_College "LeMoyne–Owen College"), and [Rhodes College](/wiki/Rhodes_College "Rhodes College").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/History_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "History of Memphis, Tennessee")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Timeline_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "Timeline of Memphis, Tennessee").

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

Occupying a substantial bluff rising from the Mississippi River, the site of Memphis has been a natural location for human settlement by varying indigenous cultures over thousands of years.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In the first millennium A.D. people of the [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture") were prominent; the culture influenced a network of communities throughout the Mississippi River Valley and its tributaries. The hierarchical societies built complexes with large earthwork ceremonial and burial mounds as expressions of their sophisticated culture.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The [Chickasaw](/wiki/Chickasaw "Chickasaw") people, believed to be their descendants, later inhabited this site and a large territory in the Southeast.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Spanish explorer [Hernando de Soto](/wiki/Hernando_de_Soto "Hernando de Soto")[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) encountered the historic Chickasaw in this area in the 16th century, followed in the 17th century by French explorers led by [René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle"),.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

J. D. L. Holmes, writing in Hudson's *Four Centuries of Southern Indians* (2007), notes that this site was a third strategic point in the late 18th century through which European powers could control United States encroachment beyond the Appalachians and their interference with Indian matters—after [Fort Nogales](/wiki/History_of_Vicksburg,_Mississippi "History of Vicksburg, Mississippi") (present-day [Vicksburg](/wiki/Vicksburg,_Mississippi "Vicksburg, Mississippi")) and [Fort Confederación](/wiki/Fort_Tombecbe "Fort Tombecbe") (present-day [Epes, Alabama](/wiki/Epes,_Alabama "Epes, Alabama")): "Chickasaw Bluffs, located on the Mississippi River at the present-day location of Memphis. Spain and the United States vied for control of this site, which was a favorite of the Chickasaws."[[19]](#cite_note-Holmes2007-19): 71

In 1795 the Spanish Governor-General of [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), [Francisco Luis Héctor de Carondelet](/wiki/Francisco_Luis_H%C3%A9ctor_de_Carondelet "Francisco Luis Héctor de Carondelet"), sent his lieutenant governor, [Manuel Gayoso de Lemos](/wiki/Manuel_Gayoso_de_Lemos "Manuel Gayoso de Lemos"), to negotiate and secure consent from the local Chickasaw so that a Spanish fort could be erected on the bluff; [Fort San Fernando De Las Barrancas](/wiki/Fort_San_Fernando_De_Las_Barrancas "Fort San Fernando De Las Barrancas") was the result.[[19]](#cite_note-Holmes2007-19): 71[[20]](#cite_note-20) Holmes notes that consent was reached despite opposition from "disappointed Americans and a pro-American faction of the Chickasaws" when the "pro-Spanish faction signed the Chickasaw Bluffs Cession and Spain provided the Chickasaws with a trading post".[[19]](#cite_note-Holmes2007-19): 71

Fort San Fernando de las Barrancas remained a focal point of Spanish activity until, as Holmes summarizes:

> [T]he Treaty of San Lorenzo or [Pinckney's Treaty](/wiki/Pinckney%27s_Treaty "Pinckney's Treaty") of 1795 [implemented in March 1797], [had as its result that] all of the careful, diplomatic work by Spanish officials in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_territory "Louisiana territory") and [West Florida](/wiki/West_Florida "West Florida"), which has succeeded for a decade in controlling the Indians [e.g., the [Choctaws](/wiki/Choctaws "Choctaws")], was undone. The United States gained the right to navigate the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and won control over the [Yazoo Strip](/wiki/Yazoo_lands "Yazoo lands") north of the thirty-first parallel.[[19]](#cite_note-Holmes2007-19): 75, 71

The Spanish dismantled the fort, shipping its lumber and iron to their locations in Arkansas.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1796, the site became the westernmost point of the newly admitted state of Tennessee, in what was then called the Southwest United States. The Chickasaw nation continued to largely occupy and control the area. Captain Isaac Guion led an American force down the Ohio River to claim the land, arriving on July 20, 1797. By this time, the Spanish had departed.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The fort's ruins went unnoticed 20 years later when Memphis was laid out as a city after the United States government paid the Chickasaw for land.[[23]](#cite_note-Patrick1990-23)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Memphis_Tennessee_1850s.jpg/250px-Memphis_Tennessee_1850s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_Tennessee_1850s.jpg)

Memphis in the mid-1850s

At the beginning of the century, as recognized by the United States in the [1786 Treaty of Hopewell](/wiki/Treaty_of_Hopewell "Treaty of Hopewell"), the land still belonged to the [Chickasaw Nation](/wiki/Chickasaw "Chickasaw"). In the [Treaty of Tuscaloosa](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tuscaloosa "Treaty of Tuscaloosa"), signed in October 1818 and ratified by Congress on January 7, 1819, the Chickasaw ceded their territory in Western Tennessee to the United States. The city of Memphis was founded less than five months after the U.S. takeover of the territory, on May 22, 1819 (incorporated December 19, 1826), by [John Overton](/wiki/John_Overton_(judge) "John Overton (judge)"), [James Winchester](/wiki/James_Winchester_(general) "James Winchester (general)") and [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson").[[24]](#cite_note-Tennessee_Encyclopedia_of_History2-24)[[25]](#cite_note-TNencyOverton-25)[[26]](#cite_note-MemLib-26) They named it after the [ancient capital](/wiki/Memphis,_Egypt "Memphis, Egypt") of Egypt on the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

From the city's foundation onwards, African Americans formed a large proportion of Memphis' population. Prior to [the abolition](/wiki/Thirteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") of [slavery in the United States](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States"), most Black people in Memphis were enslaved, being used as [forced labor](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") by white enslavers along the river or on outlying [cotton plantations](/wiki/Plantation_complexes_in_the_Southern_United_States "Plantation complexes in the Southern United States") in the [Mississippi Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_Delta "Mississippi Delta"). The city's demographics changed dramatically in the 1850s and 1860s due to waves of immigration and domestic migration. Due to increased immigration since the 1840s and the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)"), Irish Americans made up 9.9% of the population in 1850, but 23.2% by 1860, when the total population was 22,623.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Walker-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Forrest_Memphis_Raid.jpg/250px-Forrest_Memphis_Raid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Forrest_Memphis_Raid.jpg)

Attack on [Irving Block](/wiki/Irving_Block_prison "Irving Block prison") by General Forrest in 1864

[Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee#History "Tennessee") seceded from the Union in June 1861, and Memphis briefly became a [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") stronghold. [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") [ironclad gunboats](/wiki/Ironclad_warship "Ironclad warship") captured it in the naval [Battle of Memphis](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Memphis "First Battle of Memphis") on June 6, 1862, and the city and state were occupied by the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") for the duration of the war. Union commanders allowed the city to maintain its civil government during most of this period but excluded [Confederate States Army](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") veterans from office. This shifted political dynamics in the city as the war went on.[[31]](#cite_note-carden-31)

The war years contributed to additional dramatic changes in the city population. The Union Army's presence attracted many [fugitive slaves](/wiki/Fugitive_slaves_in_the_United_States "Fugitive slaves in the United States") who had escaped from surrounding rural plantations. So many sought protection behind Union lines that the Army set up [contraband camps](/wiki/Contraband_(American_Civil_War) "Contraband (American Civil War)") to accommodate them. Memphis's black population increased from 3,000 in 1860, when the total population was 22,623, to nearly 20,000 in 1865, with most settling south of the city limits.[[32]](#cite_note-Ryan-32)

### Postwar years, Reconstruction and Democratic control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Postwar years, Reconstruction and Democratic control")]

The rapid demographic changes added to the stress of war and occupation and uncertainty about who was in charge, increasing tensions between the city's ethnic Irish policemen and black Union soldiers after the war.[[31]](#cite_note-carden-31) In three days of rioting in early May 1866, the [Memphis Riots](/wiki/Memphis_Riots_of_1866 "Memphis Riots of 1866") erupted, in which white mobs made up of policemen, firemen, and other mostly ethnic Irish Americans attacked and killed 46 blacks, wounding 75 and injuring 100; raped several women; and destroyed nearly 100 houses while severely damaging churches and schools in South Memphis. The riots left much of the black settlement in ruins. Two whites were killed in the riot.[[32]](#cite_note-Ryan-32) Many blacks permanently fled Memphis afterward, especially as the [Freedmen's Bureau](/wiki/Freedmen%27s_Bureau "Freedmen's Bureau") continued to have difficulty in protecting them. Their population fell to about 15,000 by 1870,[[31]](#cite_note-carden-31) 37.4% of the total population of 40,226.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Memphis_airview_1870.jpg/250px-Memphis_airview_1870.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_airview_1870.jpg)

Historic aerial view of Memphis, 1870

Historian Barrington Walker suggests that the Irish rioted against blacks because of their relatively recent arrival as immigrants and the uncertain nature of their claim to "whiteness"; they were trying to distinguish themselves from blacks in the underclass. The main fighting participants were ethnic Irish, decommissioned black Union soldiers, and newly emancipated African-American [freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen"). Walker suggests that most of the mob was not in direct economic conflict with the blacks, as by then the Irish had attained better jobs but were establishing social and political dominance over the freedmen.[[30]](#cite_note-Walker-30)

Unlike the disturbances in some other cities, ex-Confederate veterans generally did not attack blacks in Memphis. As a result of the riots in Memphis, and a similar one in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), Louisiana in September, Congress passed the [Reconstruction Act](/wiki/Reconstruction_Act "Reconstruction Act") and the [Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_U.S._Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution").[[32]](#cite_note-Ryan-32)

### Yellow fever epidemics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Yellow fever epidemics")]

Main article: [Lower Mississippi Valley yellow fever epidemic of 1878](/wiki/Lower_Mississippi_Valley_yellow_fever_epidemic_of_1878 "Lower Mississippi Valley yellow fever epidemic of 1878")

In the 1870s, a series of [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever") epidemics devastated Memphis, with the disease carried by river passengers traveling by ships along the waterways. During the Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1878, more than 5,000 people were listed in the official register of deaths between July 26 and November 27. The vast majority of these deaths were from yellow fever, making the epidemic in the city of 40,000 one of the most traumatic and severe in urban U.S. history. Within four days of the Memphis Board of Health's declaration of a yellow fever outbreak, 20,000 residents fled the city. The ensuing panic left the poverty-stricken, the working classes, and the African-American community at the most risk from the epidemic. Those who remained relied on volunteers from religious and physician organizations to tend to the sick. Memphis confirmed more than 5,000 deaths by the year's end. The New Orleans health board listed "not less than 4,600" dead. The Mississippi Valley recorded 120,000 cases of yellow fever, with 20,000 deaths. The epidemic's $15 million in losses bankrupted Memphis, leading the state legislature to revoke its charter.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/AmCyc_Memphis_%28Tennessee%29.jpg/250px-AmCyc_Memphis_%28Tennessee%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AmCyc_Memphis_(Tennessee).jpg)

Woodcut representing the waterfront of Memphis, c. 1879

By 1870, Memphis's population of 40,000 was almost double that of Nashville and Atlanta, and it was the second-largest city in the South after New Orleans.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) Its population continued to grow after 1870, even when the [Panic of 1873](/wiki/Panic_of_1873 "Panic of 1873") hit the US hard, particularly in the South. The Panic of 1873 resulted in expanding Memphis's underclasses amid the poverty and hardship it wrought, giving further credence to Memphis as a rough, shiftless city. Leading up to the outbreak in 1878, it had suffered two[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Memphis,_Tennessee#Only_two? "Talk:Memphis, Tennessee")*] yellow fever epidemics, [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"), and [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"), giving it a reputation as sickly and filthy. It was unheard of for a city with a population as large as Memphis's not to have any waterworks; the city still relied for supplies entirely on collecting water from the river and rain cisterns and had no way to remove sewage.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) The combination of a swelling population, especially of lower and working classes, and abysmal health and sanitary conditions made Memphis ripe for a serious epidemic.

Kate Bionda, an owner of an Italian "snack house," died of a fever on August 13, 1878.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) Hers was officially reported by the Board of Health, on August 14, as the first case of yellow fever in the city.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) A massive panic ensued. The same trains and steamboats that had brought thousands into Memphis in five days carried away more than 25,000 refugees, more than half of the city's population.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) On August 23, the Board of Health finally declared a yellow fever epidemic in Memphis, and the city collapsed, hemorrhaging its population. In July of that year, the city had a population of 47,000; by September, 19,000 remained, and 17,000 of them had yellow fever.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) The only people left in the city were the lower classes, such as German and Irish immigrant workers and African Americans. None had the means to flee the city, as did the middle- and upper-class whites of Memphis, and thus they were subjected to a city of death.

Charles G. Fisher immediately formed a Citizen's Relief Committee following the Board of Health's declaration. It organized the city into refugee camps. The committee's main priority was to separate the poor from the city and isolate them in refugee camps.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) The Howard Association, formed specifically for yellow fever epidemics in New Orleans and Memphis, organized nurses and doctors in Memphis and throughout the country.[[34]](#cite_note-Hicks,_Mildred_1964-34) They stayed at the [Peabody Hotel](/wiki/Peabody_Hotel "Peabody Hotel"), the only hotel to keep its doors open during the epidemic. From there they were assigned to their respective districts. Physicians of the epidemic reported seeing as many as 100 to 150 patients daily.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33)

The Episcopal Community of St. Mary at St. Mary's Episcopal Cathedral[[35]](#cite_note-35) played an important role during the epidemic in caring for the lower classes. Already supporting a girls' school and church orphanage, the Sisters of St. Mary also sought to provide care for the Canfield Asylum, a home for black children. Each day, they alternated caring for the orphans at St. Mary's, delivering children to the Canfield Asylum, and taking soup and medicine on house calls to patients.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) Between September 9 and October 4, Sister Constance and three other nuns fell victim to the epidemic and died. They later became known as the Martyrs of Memphis.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

At long last, on October 28, a killing frost struck. The city sent out word to Memphians scattered all over the country to come home. Though yellow fever cases were recorded in the pages of Elmwood Cemetery's burial record as late as February 29, 1874, the epidemic seemed quieted.[[33]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Molly_Caldwell_2006-33) The Board of Health declared the epidemic at an end after it had caused over 20,000 deaths and financial losses of nearly $200 million.[[37]](#cite_note-Ellis,_John_H_1992-37) On November 27, a general citizen's meeting was called at the Greenlaw Opera House to offer thanks to those who had stayed behind to serve, of whom many had died. Over the next year property tax revenues collapsed, and the city could not make payments on its municipal debts. As a result, Memphis temporarily lost its city charter and was reclassified by the state legislature as a Taxing District from 1879 to 1893.[[1]](#cite_note-dissolved-1)[[2]](#cite_note-recharter-2)[[34]](#cite_note-Hicks,_Mildred_1964-34) But a new era of sanitation was developed in the city, and a new municipal government in 1879 helped form the first regional health organization, and during the 1880s Memphis led the nation in sanitary reform and improvements.[[37]](#cite_note-Ellis,_John_H_1992-37)

Perhaps the most significant effect of yellow fever on Memphis was in demographic changes. Nearly all of Memphis's upper and middle classes vanished, depriving the city of its general leadership and class structure that dictated everyday life, similar to that in other large Southern cities, such as [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), [Charleston](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina"), and [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"). In Memphis, the poorer whites and blacks fundamentally made up the city and played the greatest role in rebuilding it. The epidemic had resulted in Memphis being a less cosmopolitan place, with an economy that served the cotton trade and a population drawn increasingly from poor white and black Southerners.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Late 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Late 19th century")]

The 1890 election was strongly contested, resulting in white opponents of the [D. P. Hadden](/w/index.php?title=D._P._Hadden&action=edit&redlink=1 "D. P. Hadden (page does not exist)") faction working to deprive them of votes by [disenfranchising blacks](/wiki/Disfranchisement_after_Reconstruction_era "Disfranchisement after Reconstruction era"). The state had enacted several laws, including the requirement of [poll taxes](/wiki/Poll_taxes "Poll taxes"), that made it more difficult for them to register to vote and served to [disenfranchise](/wiki/Disfranchisement_after_Reconstruction_era "Disfranchisement after Reconstruction era") many blacks. Although political party factions in the future sometimes paid [poll taxes](/wiki/Poll_tax_(United_States) "Poll tax (United States)") to enable blacks to vote, African Americans lost their last positions on the city council in this election and were forced out of the police force. (They did not recover the ability to exercise the franchise until after the passage of civil rights legislation in the mid-1960s.) Historian L. B. Wrenn suggests the heightened political hostility of the Democratic contest and related social tensions contributed to a white mob [lynching three black grocers in Memphis in 1892](/wiki/Peoples_Grocery "Peoples Grocery").[[39]](#cite_note-wrenn-39): 124, 131

Journalist [Ida B. Wells](/wiki/Ida_B._Wells "Ida B. Wells") of Memphis investigated the lynchings, as one of the men killed was a friend of hers. She demonstrated that these and other lynchings were more often due to economic and social competition than any criminal offenses by black men. Her findings were considered so controversial and aroused so much anger that she was forced to move away from the city. But she continued to investigate and publish the abuses of [lynching](/wiki/Lynching_in_the_United_States "Lynching in the United States").[[39]](#cite_note-wrenn-39): 131

Businessmen were eager to increase the city population after the losses of 1878–79, and supported the annexation of new areas; this measure was passed in 1890 before the census. The annexation measure was finally approved by the state legislature through a compromise achieved with real estate magnates, and the area annexed was slightly smaller than first proposed.[[39]](#cite_note-wrenn-39): 126

In 1893 the city was rechartered with [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule"), which restored its ability to enact taxes. The state legislature established a cap rate.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Although the commission government was retained and enlarged to five commissioners, Democratic politicians regained control from the business elite. The commission form of government was believed to be effective in getting things done, but because all positions were elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large"), requiring them to gain majority votes, this practice reduced representation by candidates representing significant minority political interests.[[39]](#cite_note-wrenn-39): 126f

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Union_Avenue.jpg/250px-Union_Avenue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Avenue.jpg)

Cotton merchants on Union Avenue (1937)

#### Business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Business")]

In terms of its economy, Memphis developed as the world's largest [spot](/wiki/Spot_market "Spot market") cotton market and the world's largest hardwood lumber market, both commodity products of the Mississippi Delta. Into the 1950s, it was also the world's largest [mule](/wiki/Mule "Mule") market. These animals were still used extensively for agriculture.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Attracting workers from Southern rural areas as well as new European immigrants, from 1900 to 1950 the city increased nearly fourfold in population, from 102,350 to 396,000 residents.[[42]](#cite_note-lollar-42)

Racist violence continued into the 20th century, with four lynchings between 1900 and the [lynching of Thomas Williams](/wiki/Lynching_of_Thomas_Williams "Lynching of Thomas Williams") in 1928.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

A Tennessee Powder Company built an explosives powder plant to make TNT and gunpowder on a 6,000-acre site in [Millington](/wiki/Millington,_Tennessee "Millington, Tennessee") in 1940. The plant was built to make smokeless gunpowder for the [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). In May 1941, [DuPont (1802–2017)](/wiki/DuPont_(1802%E2%80%932017) "DuPont (1802–2017)") took over the plant, changed the name to the Chickasaw Ordnance Works, and produced powder for the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces"). There were 8,000 employees. The plant was dismantled after the war in 1946.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Politics: The Crump Machine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Politics: The Crump Machine")]

From the 1910s to about 1950, Memphis politics was controlled by [E. H. "Boss" Crump](/wiki/E._H._Crump "E. H. Crump"). Crump worked with the city's middle-class progressive leadership. He built up the police and fire departments—and for decades routinely called on off-duty policemen and firemen to help him out on election day. He built up public schools and reorganized city government for more efficiency. Most progressives in the South were hostile to African American interests, but not Crump. He worked with the leading Black politician [Robert Church Jr.](/wiki/Robert_Church_Jr. "Robert Church Jr.") until 1940, as well as key Black ministers. Crump raised funds from white businessmen that paid the poll taxes for Blacks, which enabled them all to vote. Crump had his limits. He avoided the demand for prohibition by Baptist activists and tolerated the city's thriving saloons and houses of prostitution.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Crump served briefly in Congress, which gave him a good view of the national picture. He was an early supporter of New York Governor [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") for the presidency in 1932. Crump strongly supported President Roosevelt in Memphis and statewide through his influence on the Congressional delegation. FDR responded by being very generous to the state, as seen in the massive [TVA](/wiki/TVA "TVA") project. He gave Crump control of Memphis patronage and most statewide patronage.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

##### City government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: City government")]

Crump secured a state law in 1911 to establish a small commission to manage the city. The city retained a form of commission government until 1967 and patronage flourished under Crump. The city installed a revolutionary sewer system and upgraded sanitation and drainage to prevent another epidemic. Pure water from an artesian well was discovered in the 1880s, securing the city's water supply. The commissioners developed an extensive network of parks and public works as part of the national [City Beautiful movement](/wiki/City_Beautiful_movement "City Beautiful movement"). Lynette Wrenn concludes the new system handled business efficiently. However, it tended toward policies that benefited the city's wealthier residents while sometimes overlooking the needs of its less affluent neighborhoods.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

#### Since 1950

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Since 1950")]

Memphis did not become a [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") city until 1963, although the state legislature had amended the constitution in 1953 to provide home rule for cities and counties. Before that, the city had to get state bills approved in order to change its charter and other policies and programs. Since 1963, it can change the charter by popular approval of the electorate.[[39]](#cite_note-wrenn-39): 194

During the 1960s, the city was at the center of the [Civil Rights Movement](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Movement "Civil Rights Movement"), as its large African-American population had been affected by state segregation practices and [disenfranchisement](/wiki/Disfranchisement_after_Reconstruction_era "Disfranchisement after Reconstruction era") in the early 20th century. African-American residents drew from the civil rights movement to improve their lives. In 1968, the [Memphis sanitation strike](/wiki/Memphis_sanitation_strike "Memphis sanitation strike") began for [living wages](/wiki/Living_wage "Living wage") and better working conditions; the workers were overwhelmingly African American. They marched to gain public awareness and support for their plight: the danger of their work and the struggles to support families with their low pay. Their drive for better pay had been met with resistance by the city government.

[Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") of the [Southern Christian Leadership Conference](/wiki/Southern_Christian_Leadership_Conference "Southern Christian Leadership Conference"), known for his leadership in the [nonviolent](/wiki/Nonviolent "Nonviolent") movement, came to lend his support to the workers' cause. King stayed at the [Lorraine Motel](/wiki/Lorraine_Motel "Lorraine Motel") in the city, and [was assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.") by [James Earl Ray](/wiki/James_Earl_Ray "James Earl Ray") on April 4, 1968, the day after giving his "[I've Been to the Mountaintop](/wiki/I%27ve_Been_to_the_Mountaintop "I've Been to the Mountaintop")" speech at the [Mason Temple](/wiki/Mason_Temple "Mason Temple").

After learning of King's murder, many African Americans in the city rioted, looting and destroying businesses and other facilities, some by arson. The governor ordered Tennessee National Guardsmen into the city within hours, where small, roving bands of rioters continued to be active.[[51]](#cite_note-lentz-51) Fearing the violence, more of the middle class began to leave the city for the suburbs.

In 1970, the Census Bureau reported Memphis's population as 60.8% white and 38.9% black.[[52]](#cite_note-census1-52) Suburbanization was attracting wealthier residents to newer housing outside the city. After the riots and court-ordered busing in 1973 to achieve desegregation of public schools, "about 40,000 of the system's 71,000 white students abandon[ed] the system in four years."[[53]](#cite_note-dillon-53) Today, the city has a majority African-American population.

Memphis is well known for its cultural contributions to the identity of the [American South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). Many renowned musicians grew up in and around Memphis and moved to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and other areas from the [Mississippi Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_Delta "Mississippi Delta"), carrying their music with them to influence other cities and listeners over radio airwaves.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Former and current Memphis residents include musicians [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis"), [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters"), [Carl Perkins](/wiki/Carl_Perkins "Carl Perkins"), [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), [Robert Johnson](/wiki/Robert_Johnson "Robert Johnson"), [W. C. Handy](/wiki/W._C._Handy "W. C. Handy"), [Bobby Whitlock](/wiki/Bobby_Whitlock "Bobby Whitlock"), [B.B. King](/wiki/B.B._King "B.B. King"), [Howlin' Wolf](/wiki/Howlin%27_Wolf "Howlin' Wolf"), [Isaac Hayes](/wiki/Isaac_Hayes "Isaac Hayes"), [Booker T. Jones](/wiki/Booker_T._Jones "Booker T. Jones"), [Eric Gales](/wiki/Eric_Gales "Eric Gales"), [Al Green](/wiki/Al_Green "Al Green"), [Alex Chilton](/wiki/Alex_Chilton "Alex Chilton"), [Three 6 Mafia](/wiki/Three_6_Mafia "Three 6 Mafia"), [the Sylvers](/wiki/The_Sylvers "The Sylvers"), [Jay Reatard](/wiki/Jay_Reatard "Jay Reatard"), [Zach Myers](/wiki/Zach_Myers "Zach Myers"), and [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin").

On December 23, 1988, a [tanker truck](/wiki/Tank_truck "Tank truck") hauling liquefied [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane") [crashed at the I-40/I-240 interchange in Midtown and exploded](/wiki/Memphis_tanker_truck_disaster "Memphis tanker truck disaster"), starting multiple vehicle and structural fires. Nine people were killed and ten were injured. It was one of Tennessee's deadliest motor vehicle accidents and eventually led to the reconstruction of the interchange where it occurred.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Memphis_Skyline_at_Night_January_2015.jpg/250px-Memphis_Skyline_at_Night_January_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_Skyline_at_Night_January_2015.jpg)

The downtown skyline at night in 2015

On June 2, 2021, the remains of Confederate General and [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") leader [Nathan Bedford Forrest](/wiki/Nathan_Bedford_Forrest "Nathan Bedford Forrest") were removed from a Memphis park.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

On January 7, 2023, after a routine traffic stop, five African American police officers brutally beat a 29-year-old African American man, [Tyre Nichols](/wiki/Tyre_Nichols "Tyre Nichols"). Nichols died from his injuries in the hospital three days later. Officer [body cam](/wiki/Body_cam "Body cam") footage and local surveillance cameras captured the altercations, which were described as "heinous" and showed "a total lack of regard for human life," according to Memphis police chief [Cerelyn "C. J." Davis](/wiki/Cerelyn_%22C._J.%22_Davis "Cerelyn \"C. J.\" Davis").[[58]](#cite_note-auto2-58) The officers were fired and charged with second-degree murder, aggravated kidnapping, and other crimes. The relatively rapid dismissal and prosecution of the offending officers were favorably perceived by Nichols's family, and Davis called it a "blueprint" for future incidents of police brutality nationwide. The incident also resulted in the disbanding of the city's "SCORPION" unit, which had been tasked with directly combating the most violent crimes in the city. All the officers charged with involvement in Nichols's death were members of the unit.[[58]](#cite_note-auto2-58)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Geography_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "Geography of Memphis, Tennessee")

See also: [List of neighborhoods in Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/List_of_neighborhoods_in_Memphis,_Tennessee "List of neighborhoods in Memphis, Tennessee")

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 324.0 square miles (839.2 km2), of which 315.1 square miles (816.0 km2) is land and 9.0 square miles (23.2 km2), or 2.76%, is water.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Memphispyramid2021.jpg/1280px-Memphispyramid2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphispyramid2021.jpg "The Downtown skyline from the lookout at the Pyramid facing southwest")

The [Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Memphis "Downtown Memphis") skyline from the lookout at the [Pyramid](/wiki/Memphis_Pyramid "Memphis Pyramid") facing southwest

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Bigriverdtme.jpg/1280px-Bigriverdtme.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bigriverdtme.jpg "Downtown from the Harahan Bridge")

[Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Memphis "Downtown Memphis") from the [Harahan Bridge](/wiki/Harahan_Bridge "Harahan Bridge")

[Downtown Memphis](/wiki/Downtown_Memphis,_Tennessee "Downtown Memphis, Tennessee") rises from a bluff along the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"). The city and metro area spread out through suburbanization, and encompass southwest Tennessee, northern [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"), and eastern [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"). Several large parks were founded in the city in the early 20th century, notably [Overton Park](/wiki/Overton_Park "Overton Park") in [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown,_Memphis "Midtown, Memphis") and the 4,500-acre (18 km2) [Shelby Farms](/wiki/Shelby_Farms "Shelby Farms"). The city is a national transportation hub and Mississippi River crossing for [Interstate 40](/wiki/Interstate_40 "Interstate 40"), (east-west), [Interstate 55](/wiki/Interstate_55 "Interstate 55") (north-south), barge traffic, Memphis International Airport ([FedEx's "SuperHub" facility](/wiki/FedEx_Express "FedEx Express")) and numerous freight railroads that serve the city.

### Riverfront

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Riverfront")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/American_Queen_Memphis_TN_2012-04-27_016.jpg/250px-American_Queen_Memphis_TN_2012-04-27_016.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_Queen_Memphis_TN_2012-04-27_016.jpg)

The [American Queen](/wiki/American_Queen "American Queen") docked at Beale Street Landing along the riverfront

The Memphis Riverfront stretches along the Mississippi River from the [Meeman-Shelby Forest State Park](/wiki/Meeman-Shelby_Forest_State_Park "Meeman-Shelby Forest State Park") in the north to the [T. O. Fuller State Park](/wiki/T._O._Fuller_State_Park "T. O. Fuller State Park") in the south. The River Walk is a [park system](/wiki/Park_system "Park system") that connects downtown Memphis from Mississippi River Greenbelt Park in the north to [Tom Lee Park](/wiki/Tom_Lee_Park "Tom Lee Park") in the south.

### Deannexation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Deannexation")]

In recent years, the city has decided to [deannex](/wiki/Municipal_deannexation_in_the_United_States "Municipal deannexation in the United States") some of its territory. It has gone through a three-phase process to deannex five areas within the city limits, returning them to unincorporated Shelby County.[[60]](#cite_note-auto-60) The first phase of deannexation occurred on January 1, 2020, when the Eads and River Bottoms areas returned to county jurisdiction. As a result, the Shelby County Sheriff is responsible for patrolling these former parts of Memphis.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The first phase of the deannexation process reduced the city's size by 5% and its population by 0.03%.[[60]](#cite_note-auto-60)

### Aquifer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Aquifer")]

Shelby County is located over four natural [aquifers](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer"), one of which is recognized as the "Memphis Sand Aquifer" or simply as the "Memphis Aquifer." Located 350 to 1,100 feet (110 to 340 m) underground, this [artesian water](/wiki/Artesian_water "Artesian water") source is considered soft and estimated by [Memphis Light, Gas and Water](/wiki/Memphis_Light,_Gas_and_Water "Memphis Light, Gas and Water") to contain more than 100 trillion US gallons (380 km3) of water.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### Cancelled Byhalia Pipeline project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cancelled Byhalia Pipeline project")]

Main article: [Byhalia Pipeline](/wiki/Byhalia_Pipeline "Byhalia Pipeline")

The Byhalia Pipeline proposed by [Plains All American Pipeline](/wiki/Plains_All_American_Pipeline "Plains All American Pipeline") and [Valero Energy](/wiki/Valero_Energy "Valero Energy"),[[63]](#cite_note-sepipeline-63) and set to begin construction in 2020,[[64]](#cite_note-cappeal1-64) was the subject of massive public and legal opposition to the project over concerns regarding possible contamination of the Memphis aquifer.[[65]](#cite_note-appipeline1-65)[[66]](#cite_note-SELChydro-66)[[67]](#cite_note-wmcactionnews517-67) Notable figures voicing public opposition to the project included Memphis Congressman [Steve Cohen](/wiki/Steve_Cohen_(politician) "Steve Cohen (politician)"), Congresswoman [Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez](/wiki/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez "Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez"), [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore"), [Danny Glover](/wiki/Danny_Glover "Danny Glover"), [Giancarlo Esposito](/wiki/Giancarlo_Esposito "Giancarlo Esposito"), and [Jane Fonda](/wiki/Jane_Fonda "Jane Fonda").[[68]](#cite_note-mlk5010-68)[[69]](#cite_note-housegov18-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The pipeline's route, which was set to run through the historic Black Boxtown neighborhood,[[63]](#cite_note-sepipeline-63) raised concerns among the projects opponents about the [racially disproportionate impacts](/wiki/Environmental_racism "Environmental racism") that contamination from the pipeline would cause if completed.[[65]](#cite_note-appipeline1-65)

Construction of the pipeline was cancelled in July 2021 after months of activism and resistance from organizations including Memphis Community Against the Pipeline (MCAP), Protect Our Aquifer, the Memphis and Mid-South Chapter of [The Climate Reality Project](/wiki/The_Climate_Reality_Project "The Climate Reality Project"), and other partnered organizations.[[68]](#cite_note-mlk5010-68)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Climate")]

Memphis has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*, [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification") *Cf*), with four distinct seasons, and is located in [USDA Plant Hardiness Zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 8a in downtown, cooling to 7b for much of the surrounding region.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Winter weather comes alternately from the upper [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), which can lead to drastic swings in temperature. Summer weather may come from [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") (very hot and humid) or the Gulf (hot and very humid). July has a daily average temperature of 82.8 °F (28.2 °C), with high levels of humidity due to moisture encroaching from the Gulf of Mexico. Afternoon and evening thunderstorms are frequent during summer but usually brief, lasting no longer than an hour. Early autumn is pleasantly drier and mild but can be hot until late October. Late autumn is rainy and cooler; precipitation peaks again in November and December. Winters are mild to chilly, with a January daily average temperature of 42.1 °F (5.6 °C). Snow occurs sporadically in winter, with an average seasonal snowfall of 2.7 inches (6.9 cm). Ice storms and freezing rain pose a greater danger, as they can often pull tree limbs down on power lines and make driving hazardous. Severe thunderstorms can occur at any time of the year, though mainly during the spring months. Large hail, strong winds, flooding, and frequent lightning can accompany these storms. Some storms spawn tornadoes.

The lowest temperature ever recorded in Memphis was −13 °F (−25 °C) on December 24, 1963,[[72]](#cite_note-Dec_Averages-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) and the highest temperature ever was 108 °F (42 °C) on July 13, 1980.[[74]](#cite_note-Jul_Averages-74) Over the course of a year, there is an average of 4.4 days of highs below freezing, 6.9 nights of lows below 20 °F (−7 °C), 43 nights of lows below freezing, 64 days of highs above 90 °F (32 °C), and 2.1 days of highs above 100 °F (38 °C).

Memphis temperatures dropped to -4 F during the [1985 North American cold wave](/wiki/1985_North_American_cold_wave "1985 North American cold wave") and during the [December 1989 United States cold wave](/wiki/December_1989_United_States_cold_wave "December 1989 United States cold wave").

Annual precipitation is high (54.94 inches [1,400 mm]) and relatively evenly distributed throughout the year. Average monthly rainfall is especially high in March through May and December, while August and September are relatively drier.
