### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Memphis had a population of 633,104. The median age was 35.0 years. 23.9% of residents were under the age of 18 and 13.9% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 89.8 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 85.8 males age 18 and over.[[87]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-90)

There were 255,642 households in Memphis, of which 29.6% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 27.3% were married-couple households, 23.3% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 42.2% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 34.6% of all households were made up of individuals and 11.0% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[87]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-90)

There were 286,713 housing units, of which 10.8% were vacant. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.2%, and the rental vacancy rate was 9.9%.[[87]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-90)

99.8% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.2% lived in rural areas.[[88]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-91)

In a reverse trend of the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)"), a growing number of African Americans are leaving Memphis to move into the suburbs of [DeSoto](/wiki/DeSoto_County,_Mississippi "DeSoto County, Mississippi") and Shelby counties.[[89]](#cite_note-demo-92) However, as of 2023, Memphis remains one of the largest majority African American cities in the nation.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Memphis_%285560473024%29.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Memphis_%285560473024%29.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Memphis_(5560473024).png)

Map of racial distribution in Memphis, 2010 U.S. Census. Each dot is 25 people: ⬤ White ⬤ Black ⬤ Asian ⬤ Hispanic ⬤ Other

As of the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census")[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit), there were 652,078 people and 245,836 households in the city.[[91]](#cite_note-94) The population density was 2,327.4 people per sq mi (898.6/km2). There were 271,552 housing units at an average density of 972.2 per square mile (375.4/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 63.33% African American, 29.39% [White](/wiki/White_American "White American"), 1.46% Asian American, 1.57% [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), 0.04% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_American "Pacific Islander American"), 1.45% from other races, and 1.04% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") people of any race were 6.49% of the population.

The median income for a household in the city was $32,285, and the median income for a family was $37,767. Males had a median income of $31,236 versus $25,183 for females. The per capita income for the city was $17,838. About 17.2% of families and 20.6% of the population were below the poverty line, including 30.1% of those under age 18, and 15.4% of those age 65 or over. In 2011, the U.S. Census Bureau ranked the Memphis area as the poorest large metro area in the country.[[92]](#cite_note-WMCTV-95) Jeff Wallace of the University of Memphis noted that the problem was related to decades of segregation in government and schools. He said that it was a low-cost job market, but other places in the world could offer cheaper labor, and the workforce was undereducated for today's challenges.[[92]](#cite_note-WMCTV-95)

The [Memphis Metropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Memphis_Metropolitan_Area "Memphis Metropolitan Area") (MSA), the [42nd largest](/wiki/List_of_United_States_metropolitan_statistical_areas_by_population "List of United States metropolitan statistical areas by population") in the United States, has a 2010 population of 1,316,100 and includes the Tennessee counties of [Shelby](/wiki/Shelby_County,_Tennessee "Shelby County, Tennessee"), [Tipton](/wiki/Tipton_County,_Tennessee "Tipton County, Tennessee") and [Fayette](/wiki/Fayette_County,_Tennessee "Fayette County, Tennessee"); as well as the northern [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi") counties of [DeSoto](/wiki/DeSoto_County,_Mississippi "DeSoto County, Mississippi"), [Marshall](/wiki/Marshall_County,_Mississippi "Marshall County, Mississippi"), [Tate](/wiki/Tate_County,_Mississippi "Tate County, Mississippi"), and [Tunica](/wiki/Tunica_County,_Mississippi "Tunica County, Mississippi"); and [Crittenden County, Arkansas](/wiki/Crittenden_County,_Arkansas "Crittenden County, Arkansas"), all part of the [Mississippi Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_Delta "Mississippi Delta").

The total metropolitan area has a higher proportion of whites and a higher per capita income than the population in the city. The 2010 census shows that the Memphis metro area is close to a [majority-minority](/wiki/Majority-minority "Majority-minority") population:

> the white population is 47.9 percent of the eight-county area's 1,316,100 residents. The non-Hispanic white population, a designation frequently used in census reports, was 46.2 percent of the total. The African American percentage was 45.7. For several decades, the Memphis metro area has had the highest percentage of black population among the nation's large metropolitan areas. The area has seemed on a path to become the nation's first metro area of one million or more with a majority black population.[[89]](#cite_note-demo-92)

The top countries of origin for Memphis' immigrants in 2015 were Mexico, India, China, Honduras and Vietnam.[[93]](#cite_note-96)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Elmwood-Asian.JPG/250px-Elmwood-Asian.JPG)](/wiki/File:Elmwood-Asian.JPG)

Asian-American tombstones in [Elmwood Cemetery](/wiki/Elmwood_Cemetery_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Elmwood Cemetery (Memphis, Tennessee)")

An 1870 map of Memphis shows religious buildings of the [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), [Congregational](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), and other [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"), and a [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") congregation.[[94]](#cite_note-97) In 2009, places of worship exist for Christians, Jews, [Hindus](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu"), [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist"), and [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim").

The international headquarters of the [Church of God in Christ](/wiki/Church_of_God_in_Christ "Church of God in Christ"), the largest [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") denomination in the United States, is located in Memphis. Its [Mason Temple](/wiki/Mason_Temple "Mason Temple") was named after the denomination's founder, [Charles Harrison Mason](/wiki/Charles_Harrison_Mason "Charles Harrison Mason"). This auditorium is where Rev. [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") gave his noted "[I've Been to the Mountaintop](/wiki/I%27ve_Been_to_the_Mountaintop "I've Been to the Mountaintop")" speech in April 1968, the night before he was assassinated at his motel. The [National Civil Rights Museum](/wiki/National_Civil_Rights_Museum "National Civil Rights Museum"), located in Memphis at the Lorraine Motel and other buildings, has an annual ceremony at Mason's [Temple of Deliverance](/wiki/Temple_of_Deliverance_Church_of_God_in_Christ "Temple of Deliverance Church of God in Christ") where it honors people with Freedom Awards.

[Bellevue Baptist Church](/wiki/Bellevue_Baptist_Church "Bellevue Baptist Church") is a [Southern Baptist](/wiki/Southern_Baptist "Southern Baptist") [megachurch](/wiki/Megachurch "Megachurch") in Memphis that was founded in 1903. Its current membership is around 30,000.[[95]](#cite_note-98) For many years, it was led by [Adrian Rogers](/wiki/Adrian_Rogers "Adrian Rogers"), a three-term president of the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention").

Other notable and/or large churches in Memphis include Second Presbyterian Church ([EPC](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(United_States) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (United States)")), Highpoint Church[[96]](#cite_note-Highpoint_Church-99) (SBC), Hope Presbyterian Church ([EPC](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(United_States) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (United States)")), Evergreen Presbyterian Church ([PCUSA](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)")), Colonial Park United Methodist Church, Christ [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church"), Idlewild Presbyterian Church (PCUSA), [GraceLife Pentecostal Church](/w/index.php?title=GraceLife_Pentecostal_Church&action=edit&redlink=1 "GraceLife Pentecostal Church (page does not exist)") ([UPCI](/wiki/United_Pentecostal_Church_International "United Pentecostal Church International")), First Baptist Broad, Temple of Deliverance, [Calvary Episcopal Church](/wiki/Calvary_Episcopal_Church_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Calvary Episcopal Church (Memphis, Tennessee)"), the [Church of the River (First Unitarian Church of Memphis)](/wiki/Church_of_the_River_(First_Unitarian_Church_of_Memphis) "Church of the River (First Unitarian Church of Memphis)"), First Congregational Church (UCC) and Annunciation Greek Orthodox Church.

Memphis is home to two cathedrals. The Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception is the seat of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Memphis](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Memphis "Roman Catholic Diocese of Memphis"), and [St. Mary's Episcopal Cathedral](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Episcopal_Cathedral_in_Memphis "St. Mary's Episcopal Cathedral in Memphis") is the seat of the [Episcopal Diocese of West Tennessee](/wiki/Episcopal_Diocese_of_West_Tennessee "Episcopal Diocese of West Tennessee").

Memphis is home to [Temple Israel](/wiki/Temple_Israel_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Temple Israel (Memphis, Tennessee)"), a [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") synagogue that has approximately 7,000 members, making it one of the largest Reform synagogues in the country. [Baron Hirsch Synagogue](/wiki/Baron_Hirsch_Synagogue "Baron Hirsch Synagogue") is the largest [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") [shul](/wiki/Shul "Shul") in the United States.[[97]](#cite_note-Goldring-Woldenberg-100) Jewish residents were part of the city before the Civil War, but more Jewish immigrants came from Eastern Europe in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Memphis is home to an estimated 10,000 to 15,000 Muslims of various cultures and ethnicities.[[98]](#cite_note-101)

A number of seminaries are located in Memphis and the metropolitan area. Memphis is home to [Memphis Theological Seminary](/wiki/Memphis_Theological_Seminary "Memphis Theological Seminary") and [Harding School of Theology](/wiki/Harding_School_of_Theology "Harding School of Theology"). Suburban Cordova is home to [Mid-America Baptist Theological Seminary](/wiki/Mid-America_Baptist_Theological_Seminary "Mid-America Baptist Theological Seminary").

### Crime and police

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Crime and police")]

Main article: [Crime in Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Crime_in_Memphis,_Tennessee "Crime in Memphis, Tennessee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Memphis_TN_2014-February_017.jpg/250px-Memphis_TN_2014-February_017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_TN_2014-February_017.jpg)

A [Memphis Police Department](/wiki/Memphis_Police_Department "Memphis Police Department") vehicle

In the 21st century, Memphis' crime rate has remained significantly higher than the national average. [Memphis' gangs](/wiki/Gangs_in_Memphis,_Tennessee "Gangs in Memphis, Tennessee") are a major reason for the crime crisis in the city.[[99]](#cite_note-102) Since the 2000s, it has consistently been recognized as one of the most dangerous cities in the U.S.[[100]](#cite_note-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104)[[102]](#cite_note-105)[[103]](#cite_note-106)

In 2023, Memphis set a homicide record with 397 homicides.[[104]](#cite_note-hom1-107) New York City, the nation's largest city with a population of 8.5 million, had a lower homicide count of 386 in 2023.[[105]](#cite_note-108) Identity theft, carjackings and robberies were also happening at a highly concerning rate in the city after 2020.[[104]](#cite_note-hom1-107) Crime was the primary reason 30,000 former Memphis residents decided to relocate outside the city between 2017 and 2022.[[106]](#cite_note-109) Memphis' businesses are also leaving the city or closing down at a high rate due to rampant crime.[[107]](#cite_note-110)[[108]](#cite_note-111) Memphis' leaders are continually discussing and implementing strategies such as recruiting more police officers to hopefully lower crime in the city.[[109]](#cite_note-112)[[110]](#cite_note-113) However, on September 9, 2025, it was revealed that crime in Memphis had in fact dropped to a 25-year low across major categories.[[111]](#cite_note-114) On September 15, 2025, president Trump signed a order to mobilize federal law enforcement agents, including FBI, and to deploy the [Tennessee National Guard](/wiki/Tennessee_National_Guard "Tennessee National Guard") to Memphis.[[112]](#cite_note-115)[[113]](#cite_note-116) Some members of the Memphis city council, including Memphis mayor [Paul Young](/wiki/Paul_Young_(American_politician) "Paul Young (American politician)"), opposed the plan.[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118) The federal government deployed the [Memphis Safe Task Force](/wiki/Memphis_Safe_Task_Force "Memphis Safe Task Force") to Memphis in October 2025.[[116]](#cite_note-119)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Economy_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "Economy of Memphis, Tennessee")

The city's central geographic location has aided its business development. On the Mississippi River and intersected by five major freight railroads and two [Interstate Highways](/wiki/Interstate_highways "Interstate highways"), I-40 and I-55, Memphis is well positioned for commerce in the transportation and shipping industry. Its access by water was key to its initial development, with steamboats plying the Mississippi river. Railroad construction strengthened its connection to other markets to the east and west.

Since the second half of the 20th century, highways and interstates have played major roles as transportation corridors. A third interstate, [I-69](/wiki/Interstate_69_in_Tennessee "Interstate 69 in Tennessee"), is under construction, and a fourth, [I-22](/wiki/Interstate_22 "Interstate 22"), has recently been designated from the former High Priority Corridor X. River barges are unloaded onto trucks and trains. The city is home to [Memphis International Airport](/wiki/Memphis_International_Airport "Memphis International Airport"), the world's [busiest cargo airport](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_cargo_traffic "World's busiest airports by cargo traffic"), surpassing [Hong Kong International Airport](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport") in 2021. Memphis serves as a primary hub for [FedEx Express](/wiki/FedEx_Express "FedEx Express") shipping.

As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit), Memphis was the home of three Fortune 500 companies: [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx") (no. 63), [International Paper](/wiki/International_Paper "International Paper") (no. 107), and [AutoZone](/wiki/AutoZone "AutoZone") (no. 306).[[117]](#cite_note-120)

Other major corporations based in Memphis include [Allenberg Cotton](/wiki/Allenberg_Cotton "Allenberg Cotton"), [American Residential Services](/wiki/American_Residential_Services "American Residential Services") (also known as ARS/Rescue Rooter); [Baker, Donelson, Bearman, Caldwell & Berkowitz](/wiki/Baker,_Donelson,_Bearman,_Caldwell_%26_Berkowitz "Baker, Donelson, Bearman, Caldwell & Berkowitz"); [Cargill Cotton](/wiki/Cargill_Cotton "Cargill Cotton"), [City Gear](/wiki/City_Gear "City Gear"), [First Horizon National Corporation](/wiki/First_Horizon_National_Corporation "First Horizon National Corporation"), [Fred's](/wiki/Fred%27s "Fred's"), [GTx](/wiki/GTx_Incorporated "GTx Incorporated"), [Lenny's Sub Shop](/wiki/Lenny%27s_Sub_Shop "Lenny's Sub Shop"), [Mid-America Apartments](/wiki/Mid-America_Apartments "Mid-America Apartments"), [Perkins Restaurant and Bakery](/wiki/Perkins_Restaurant_and_Bakery "Perkins Restaurant and Bakery"), [ServiceMaster](/wiki/ServiceMaster "ServiceMaster"), [True Temper Sports](/wiki/True_Temper_Sports "True Temper Sports"), [Varsity Brands](/wiki/Varsity_Brands "Varsity Brands"), and [Verso Paper](/wiki/Verso_Paper "Verso Paper"). Corporations with major operations based in Memphis include [Gibson guitars](/wiki/Gibson_guitars "Gibson guitars") (based in Nashville) and [Smith & Nephew](/wiki/Smith_%26_Nephew "Smith & Nephew").

The [Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis") also has a [branch in Memphis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis_Memphis_Branch "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis Memphis Branch").

The entertainment and film industries have discovered Memphis in recent years. Several major motion pictures, most of which were recruited and assisted by the Memphis & Shelby County Film and Television Commission,[[118]](#cite_note-121) have been filmed in Memphis, including *[Making the Grade](/wiki/Making_the_Grade_(1984_film) "Making the Grade (1984 film)")* (1984), *[Elvis and Me](/wiki/Elvis_and_Me "Elvis and Me")* (1988), *[Great Balls of Fire!](/wiki/Great_Balls_of_Fire!_(film) "Great Balls of Fire! (film)")* (1988), *[Heart of Dixie](/wiki/Heart_of_Dixie_(film) "Heart of Dixie (film)")* (1989), *[Mystery Train](/wiki/Mystery_Train_(film) "Mystery Train (film)")* (1989), *[The Silence of the Lambs](/wiki/The_Silence_of_the_Lambs_(film) "The Silence of the Lambs (film)")* (1991), *[Trespass](/wiki/Trespass_(1992_film) "Trespass (1992 film)")* (1992), *[The Gun in Betty Lou's Handbag](/wiki/The_Gun_in_Betty_Lou%27s_Handbag "The Gun in Betty Lou's Handbag")* (1992), *[The Firm](/wiki/The_Firm_(1993_film) "The Firm (1993 film)")* (1993), *[The Delta](/wiki/The_Delta_(film) "The Delta (film)")* (1996), *[The People Vs. Larry Flynt](/wiki/The_People_Vs._Larry_Flynt "The People Vs. Larry Flynt")* (1996), *[The Rainmaker](/wiki/The_Rainmaker_(1997_film) "The Rainmaker (1997 film)")* (1997), *[Cast Away](/wiki/Cast_Away "Cast Away")* (2000), *[21 Grams](/wiki/21_Grams "21 Grams")* (2002), *[A Painted House](/wiki/A_Painted_House "A Painted House")* (2002), *[Hustle & Flow](/wiki/Hustle_%26_Flow "Hustle & Flow")* (2005), *[Forty Shades of Blue](/wiki/Forty_Shades_of_Blue "Forty Shades of Blue")* (2005), *[Walk the Line](/wiki/Walk_the_Line "Walk the Line")* (2005), *[Black Snake Moan](/wiki/Black_Snake_Moan_(film) "Black Snake Moan (film)")* (2007), *[Nothing But the Truth](/wiki/Nothing_but_the_Truth_(2008_American_film) "Nothing but the Truth (2008 American film)")* (2008), *[Soul Men](/wiki/Soul_Men "Soul Men")* (2008), and *[The Grace Card](/wiki/The_Grace_Card "The Grace Card")* (2011). *[The Blind Side](/wiki/The_Blind_Side_(film) "The Blind Side (film)")* (2009) was set in Memphis but filmed in Atlanta. The 1992 television movie *Memphis*, starring Memphis native [Cybill Shepherd](/wiki/Cybill_Shepherd "Cybill Shepherd"), who also served as executive producer and writer, was also filmed in Memphis.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Culture_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "Culture of Memphis, Tennessee")

### Cultural events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cultural events")]

One of the largest celebrations of the city is [Memphis in May](/wiki/Memphis_in_May "Memphis in May"). The month-long series of events promotes Memphis's heritage and outreach of its people far beyond the city's borders. The four main events are the [Beale Street Music Festival](/wiki/Beale_Street_Music_Festival "Beale Street Music Festival"), International Week, The World Championship Barbecue Cooking Contest, and the Great River Run. The World Championship Barbecue Cooking Contest is the largest pork [barbecue](/wiki/Barbecue "Barbecue")-cooking contest in the world.

In April, downtown Memphis celebrates the "Africa in April Cultural Awareness Festival," or simply Africa in April. The festival was designed to celebrate the arts, history, culture, and diversity of the [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora"). Africa in April is a three-day festival with vendors' markets, fashion showcases, blues showcases, and an international diversity parade.[[119]](#cite_note-africainapril-122)

During late May-early June, Memphis is home to the [Memphis Italian Festival](/wiki/Memphis_Italian_Festival "Memphis Italian Festival") at Marquette Park. The 2019 festival will be its 30th and has hosted musical acts, local artisans, and Italian cooking competitions. It also presents chef demonstrations, the Coors Light Competitive [Bocce](/wiki/Bocce "Bocce") Tournament, the Galtelli Cup Recreational Bocce Tournament, a volleyball tournament, and pizza-tossing demonstrations. This festival was started by Holy Rosary School and Parish and began inside the School parking lot in 1989. The Memphis Italian Festival is run almost completely by former and current Holy Rosary School and Church members and begins with a 5K run each year.

[Carnival Memphis](/wiki/Carnival_Memphis "Carnival Memphis"), formerly known as the Memphis Cotton Carnival, is an annual series of parties and festivities in June that salutes various aspects of Memphis and its industries. An annual King and Queen of Carnival are secretly selected to reign over Carnival activities. From 1935 to 1982, the African-American community staged the Cotton Makers Jubilee; it has merged with Carnival Memphis.[[120]](#cite_note-123)

A market and arts festival, the Cooper-Young Festival,[[121]](#cite_note-124) is held annually in September in the [Cooper-Young](/wiki/Cooper-Young,_Memphis "Cooper-Young, Memphis") district of [Midtown Memphis](/wiki/Midtown_Memphis "Midtown Memphis"). The event draws artists from all over North America and includes local music, art sales, contests, and displays.

Memphis sponsors several film festivals: the [Indie Memphis Film Festival](/wiki/Indie_Memphis_Film_Festival "Indie Memphis Film Festival"), Outflix, and the Memphis International Film and Music Festival. The Indie Memphis Film Festival is in its 14th year and was held April 27–28, 2013.[[122]](#cite_note-125) Recognized by *MovieMaker Magazine* as one of 25 "Coolest Film Festivals" (2009) and one of 25 "Festivals Worth the Entry Fee" (2011), Indie Memphis offers Memphis year-round independent film programming, including the Global Lens international film series, IM Student Shorts student films, and an outdoor concert film series at the historic [Levitt Shell](/wiki/Levitt_Shell "Levitt Shell"). The Outflix Film Festival, also in its 15th year, was held September 7–13, 2013. Outflix features a full week of [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") cinema, including short films, features, and documentaries. The Memphis International Film and Music Festival is held in April; it is in its 11th year and takes place at Malco's Ridgeway Four.

[Mid-South Pride](/wiki/Mid-South_Pride "Mid-South Pride") is Tennessee's second-largest [LGBT pride](/wiki/LGBT_pride "LGBT pride") event.[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-127)

On the weekend before Thanksgiving, the Memphis International Jazz Festival is held in the South Main Historic Arts District in Downtown Memphis. This festival promotes the important role Memphis has played in shaping Jazz nationally and internationally. Acts such as George Coleman, Herman Green, Kirk Whalum and Marvin Stamm all come out of the rich musical heritage in Memphis.

Formerly titled the [W. C. Handy](/wiki/W._C._Handy "W. C. Handy") Awards, the International Blues Awards are presented by the [Blues Foundation](/wiki/Blues_Foundation "Blues Foundation") (headquartered in Memphis) for [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") music achievement. Weeklong playing competitions are held, as well as an awards banquet including a night of performance and celebration.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Music")]

Memphis is the home of founders and pioneers of various American music genres, including [Memphis soul](/wiki/Memphis_soul "Memphis soul"), [Memphis blues](/wiki/Memphis_blues "Memphis blues"), [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [rock n' roll](/wiki/Rock_n%27_roll "Rock n' roll"), [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly"), [Memphis rap](/wiki/Memphis_rap "Memphis rap"), [Buck](/wiki/Gangsta_walking "Gangsta walking"), [crunk](/wiki/Crunk "Crunk"), and "sharecropper" [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") (in contrast to the "[rhinestone](/wiki/Rhinestone "Rhinestone")" country sound historically associated with [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee")).

Many musicians, including [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis"), [Carl Perkins](/wiki/Carl_Perkins "Carl Perkins"), [Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison"), [Booker T. & the M.G.'s](/wiki/Booker_T._%26_the_M.G.%27s "Booker T. & the M.G.'s"), [Otis Redding](/wiki/Otis_Redding "Otis Redding"), [Isaac Hayes](/wiki/Isaac_Hayes "Isaac Hayes"), [Shawn Lane](/wiki/Shawn_Lane "Shawn Lane"), [Al Green](/wiki/Al_Green "Al Green"), [Bobby Whitlock](/wiki/Bobby_Whitlock "Bobby Whitlock"), [Rance Allen](/wiki/Rance_Allen "Rance Allen"), [Percy Sledge](/wiki/Percy_Sledge "Percy Sledge"), [Solomon Burke](/wiki/Solomon_Burke "Solomon Burke"), [William Bell](/wiki/William_Bell_(singer) "William Bell (singer)"), [Sam & Dave](/wiki/Sam_%26_Dave "Sam & Dave") and [B.B. King](/wiki/B.B._King "B.B. King"), got their start in Memphis in the 1950s and 1960s.

[Beale Street](/wiki/Beale_Street "Beale Street") is a national historical landmark and shows the impact Memphis has had on American [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), particularly after World War II as electric guitars took precedence over the original acoustic sound from the [Mississippi Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_Delta "Mississippi Delta"). [Sam Phillips](/wiki/Sam_Phillips "Sam Phillips")' [Sun Studio](/wiki/Sun_Studio "Sun Studio") still stands and is open for tours. Elvis, Johnny Cash, Jerry Lee Lewis, Carl Perkins and Roy Orbison all made their first recordings there and were "discovered" by Phillips. Many great blues artists recorded there, such as [W. C. Handy](/wiki/W._C._Handy "W. C. Handy"), the "Father of the Blues."

[Stax Records](/wiki/Stax_Records "Stax Records") created a classic 1960s [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") sound, much grittier and horn-based than the better-known [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown") from Detroit. Booker T. and the M.G.'s were the label's backing band for most of the classic hits that came from Stax, by Sam & Dave, [Otis Redding](/wiki/Otis_Redding "Otis Redding"), [Wilson Pickett](/wiki/Wilson_Pickett "Wilson Pickett") and many more. The sound was revisited in the 1980s in the [*Blues Brothers* movie](/wiki/The_Blues_Brothers_(film) "The Blues Brothers (film)"), in which many of the musicians starred as themselves.

Memphis is also noted for its influence on the [power pop](/wiki/Power_pop "Power pop") musical genre in the 1970s. Notable bands and musicians include [Big Star](/wiki/Big_Star "Big Star"), [Chris Bell](/wiki/Chris_Bell_(American_musician) "Chris Bell (American musician)"), [Alex Chilton](/wiki/Alex_Chilton "Alex Chilton"), [Tommy Hoehn](/wiki/Tommy_Hoehn "Tommy Hoehn"), [The Scruffs](/wiki/The_Scruffs "The Scruffs") and [Prix](/wiki/Prix "Prix").[[125]](#cite_note-flyer-128)[[126]](#cite_note-reporter-129)

[Memphis rap](/wiki/Memphis_rap "Memphis rap") culture significantly influenced hip-hop culture worldwide. Memphis hip-hop became more mainstream during the 2000s. Memphis-based artists such as [Three 6 Mafia](/wiki/Three_6_Mafia "Three 6 Mafia"), [Juicy J](/wiki/Juicy_J "Juicy J"), [Lil Wyte](/wiki/Lil_Wyte "Lil Wyte"), [8Ball & MJG](/wiki/8Ball_%26_MJG "8Ball & MJG"), [Gangsta Boo](/wiki/Gangsta_Boo "Gangsta Boo"), [Project Pat](/wiki/Project_Pat "Project Pat"), [La Chat](/wiki/La_Chat "La Chat"), [Young Dolph](/wiki/Young_Dolph "Young Dolph"), [Yo Gotti](/wiki/Yo_Gotti "Yo Gotti"), [NLE Choppa](/wiki/NLE_Choppa "NLE Choppa"), [Moneybagg Yo](/wiki/Moneybagg_Yo "Moneybagg Yo"), [GloRilla](/wiki/GloRilla "GloRilla"), [Pooh Shiesty](/wiki/Pooh_Shiesty "Pooh Shiesty"), and [Key Glock](/wiki/Key_Glock "Key Glock") is among the most popular emcees in the nation.[[127]](#cite_note-130)[[128]](#cite_note-131)[[129]](#cite_note-132)

Several notable singers are from the Memphis area, including [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake"), [K. Michelle](/wiki/K._Michelle "K. Michelle"), [Kirk Whalum](/wiki/Kirk_Whalum "Kirk Whalum"), [Ruth Welting](/wiki/Ruth_Welting "Ruth Welting"), [Kid Memphis](/wiki/Kid_Memphis "Kid Memphis"), [Kallen Esperian](/wiki/Kallen_Esperian "Kallen Esperian"), [Julien Baker](/wiki/Julien_Baker "Julien Baker"), and [Andrew VanWyngarden](/wiki/Andrew_VanWyngarden "Andrew VanWyngarden"). The [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera") of New York had its first tour in Memphis in 1906; in the 1990s it decided to tour only larger cities. Metropolitan Opera performances are now broadcast in HD at local movie theaters across the country.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Memphis is the home of [Memphis-style barbecue](/wiki/Memphis-style_barbecue "Memphis-style barbecue"), which is one of four predominant regional styles of barbecue in the United States. Memphis-style barbecue has become well known due to the [World Championship Barbecue Cooking Contest](/wiki/Memphis_in_May#The_World_Championship_Barbecue_Cooking_Contest "Memphis in May") held each May, which has been listed in *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as the largest pork barbecue contest in the world. Notable Memphis restaurants include:

* Alcenia's, a soul food restaurant that has been featured on Food Network and the Travel Channel[[130]](#cite_note-133)
* Charlie Vergos' Rendezvous, founded in 1948, this barbecue restaurant located in an alley has been visited by countless celebrities[[131]](#cite_note-134)
* Chef Tam's Underground Cafe, operated by Chef Tamra Patterson, winner of [Guy's Grocery Games](/wiki/Guy%27s_Grocery_Games "Guy's Grocery Games") in 2018 and [Chopped](/wiki/Chopped_(TV_series) "Chopped (TV series)") in 2022[[132]](#cite_note-135)
* Dyer's Burgers, which has used the same grease to deep-fry their burgers for over 100 years[[133]](#cite_note-136)
* Earnestine and Hazel's, a historic dive bar visited previously by the likes of [B.B. King](/wiki/B.B._King "B.B. King"), [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), and [Tina Turner](/wiki/Tina_Turner "Tina Turner")[[134]](#cite_note-137)
* In addition to barbecue, the cuisine of Memphis is also defined by:
  + [Fried chicken](/wiki/Fried_chicken "Fried chicken"), such as that from Gus's World Famous Fried Chicken, a restaurant founded in nearby [Mason, Tennessee](/wiki/Mason,_Tennessee "Mason, Tennessee") in 1953 and has since expanded to over 35 locations
  + [Chicken wings](/wiki/Chicken_wings_as_food "Chicken wings as food"), served with Honey Gold, a sweet and spicy sauce made with honey, mustard, and cayenne

### Visual art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Visual art")]

In addition to the [Brooks Museum](/wiki/Memphis_Brooks_Museum_of_Art "Memphis Brooks Museum of Art") and [Dixon Gallery and Gardens](/wiki/Dixon_Gallery_and_Gardens "Dixon Gallery and Gardens"), Memphis plays host to two burgeoning visual art areas, one city-sanctioned and the other organically formed.

The South Main Arts District is an arts neighborhood in south downtown. Over the past 20 years, the area has morphed from a derelict brothel and [juke joint](/wiki/Juke_joint "Juke joint") neighborhood to a [gentrified](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), well-lit area sponsoring "Trolley Night," when arts patrons stroll down the street to see fire spinners, DJs playing in front of clubs, specialty shops and galleries.[[135]](#cite_note-138)[[136]](#cite_note-139) Not far from the South Main Arts district is [Medicine Factory](/wiki/Medicine_Factory "Medicine Factory"), an artist-run organization.[[137]](#cite_note-140)

Another developing arts district in Memphis is Broad Avenue. This east–west avenue is undergoing neighborhood revitalization from the influx of craft and visual artists taking up residence and studios in the area.[[138]](#cite_note-141)[[139]](#cite_note-142) An art professor from [Rhodes College](/wiki/Rhodes_College "Rhodes College") holds small openings on the first floor of his home for local students and professional artists. Odessa, another art space on Broad Avenue, hosts student art shows and local electronic music. Other gallery spaces spring up for semi-annual artwalks.[[140]](#cite_note-143)[[141]](#cite_note-144)

Memphis also has non-commercial visual arts organizations and spaces, including local painter Pinkney Herbert's Marshall Arts gallery, on Marshall Avenue near [Sun Studios](/wiki/Sun_Studios "Sun Studios"), another arts neighborhood characterized by affordable rent.[[142]](#cite_note-145)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Literature")]

Well-known writers from Memphis include [Shelby Foote](/wiki/Shelby_Foote "Shelby Foote"), the noted [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") historian. Novelist [John Grisham](/wiki/John_Grisham "John Grisham") grew up in nearby [DeSoto County, Mississippi](/wiki/DeSoto_County,_Mississippi "DeSoto County, Mississippi"), and sets many of his books in Memphis.

Many works of fiction and literature are set in Memphis. These include *[The Reivers](/wiki/The_Reivers "The Reivers")* by [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner") (1962), *September, September* by Shelby Foote (1977); [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Matthew_Hillsman_Taylor "Peter Matthew Hillsman Taylor")'s *The Old Forest and Other Stories* (1985), and his [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize")-winning *[A Summons to Memphis](/wiki/A_Summons_to_Memphis "A Summons to Memphis")* (1986); *[The Firm](/wiki/The_Firm_(novel) "The Firm (novel)")* (1991) and *[The Client](/wiki/The_Client_(novel) "The Client (novel)")* (1993), both by [John Grisham](/wiki/John_Grisham "John Grisham"); *Memphis Afternoons: a Memoir* by James Conaway (1993); *Plague of Dreamers* by Steve Stern (1997); *[Cassina Gambrel Was Missing](/wiki/Cassina_Gambrel_Was_Missing "Cassina Gambrel Was Missing")* by William Watkins (1999); *The Guardian* by Beecher Smith (1999); "We are Billion-Year-Old Carbon" by Corey Mesler (2005); *[The Silence of the Lambs](/wiki/The_Silence_of_the_Lambs_(novel) "The Silence of the Lambs (novel)")* by [Thomas Harris](/wiki/Thomas_Harris "Thomas Harris"); and *The Architect* by James Williamson (2007).

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Tourism_in_Memphis,_Tennessee "Tourism in Memphis, Tennessee")

#### Points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Peabody_Hotel_Memphis.jpg/250px-Peabody_Hotel_Memphis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peabody_Hotel_Memphis.jpg)

[Peabody Hotel](/wiki/Peabody_Hotel "Peabody Hotel")

* [Beale Street](/wiki/Beale_Street "Beale Street") – a significant location in the city's history, as well as in the history of the [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"). Street performers play live music, and bars and clubs feature live entertainment.
* [Graceland](/wiki/Graceland "Graceland") – the private residence of [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley")
* [Memphis Zoo](/wiki/Memphis_Zoo "Memphis Zoo") – features exhibits of mammals, birds, fish, and amphibians
* [Peabody Hotel](/wiki/Peabody_Hotel "Peabody Hotel") – known for the "Peabody Ducks" on the hotel rooftop
* [Sun Studio](/wiki/Sun_Studio "Sun Studio") – a recording studio opened in 1950; it now also contains a museum
* [Orpheum Theatre](/wiki/Orpheum_Theatre_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Orpheum Theatre (Memphis, Tennessee)") – features Broadway shows, [Ballet Memphis](/wiki/Ballet_Memphis "Ballet Memphis") and [Opera Memphis](/wiki/Opera_Memphis "Opera Memphis")
* [The New Daisy Theatre](/wiki/The_New_Daisy_Theatre "The New Daisy Theatre") – concert venue located on [Beale Street](/wiki/Beale_Street "Beale Street")
* Mud Island Amphitheatre – concert venue[[143]](#cite_note-146)
* [Memphis Pyramid](/wiki/Memphis_Pyramid "Memphis Pyramid") – location of the largest [Bass Pro Shops](/wiki/Bass_Pro_Shops "Bass Pro Shops") in the world, an observation deck, restaurants, bowling alley, aquarium, and hotel[[144]](#cite_note-147)

Other Memphis attractions include the [Simmons Bank Liberty Stadium](/wiki/Simmons_Bank_Liberty_Stadium "Simmons Bank Liberty Stadium"), [FedExForum](/wiki/FedExForum "FedExForum"), and Mississippi riverboat day cruises.

#### Museums and art collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Museums and art collections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/The_Lorraine_Motel%2C_site_of_the_Martin_Luther_King_assassination_and_the_National_Civil_Rights_Museum..jpg/250px-The_Lorraine_Motel%2C_site_of_the_Martin_Luther_King_assassination_and_the_National_Civil_Rights_Museum..jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Lorraine_Motel,_site_of_the_Martin_Luther_King_assassination_and_the_National_Civil_Rights_Museum..jpg)

[National Civil Rights Museum](/wiki/National_Civil_Rights_Museum "National Civil Rights Museum") at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Memphis_Brooks_Museum_of_Art.jpg/250px-Memphis_Brooks_Museum_of_Art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_Brooks_Museum_of_Art.jpg)

[Memphis Brooks Museum of Art](/wiki/Memphis_Brooks_Museum_of_Art "Memphis Brooks Museum of Art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Mud_island_river_park.jpg/250px-Mud_island_river_park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mud_island_river_park.jpg)

[Mud Island](/wiki/Mud_Island,_Memphis "Mud Island, Memphis") Mississippi River Park

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Stax_Museum_%26_Satellite_Record_Shop.jpg/250px-Stax_Museum_%26_Satellite_Record_Shop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stax_Museum_%26_Satellite_Record_Shop.jpg)

[Stax Museum](/wiki/Stax_Museum_of_American_Soul_Music "Stax Museum of American Soul Music") and Satellite Record Shop

* [National Civil Rights Museum](/wiki/National_Civil_Rights_Museum "National Civil Rights Museum") – located in the [Lorraine Motel](/wiki/Lorraine_Motel "Lorraine Motel") and related buildings, where [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") was assassinated. It includes a historical overview of the American civil rights movement and an interpretation of historic and current issues.
* [Memphis Brooks Museum of Art](/wiki/Memphis_Brooks_Museum_of_Art "Memphis Brooks Museum of Art") – the oldest and largest fine art museum in Tennessee;[[145]](#cite_note-brooksmuseum1-148) the collection includes [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque"), [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"), and 20th-century artists.
* [Belz Museum of Asian and Judaic Art](/wiki/Belz_Museum_of_Asian_and_Judaic_Art "Belz Museum of Asian and Judaic Art") – contains a large collection of Asian [jade](/wiki/Jade "Jade") art, Asian art, and Judaic art.
* [Dixon Gallery and Gardens](/wiki/Dixon_Gallery_and_Gardens "Dixon Gallery and Gardens") – focuses on French and American impressionism and contains the Stout Collection of 18th-century German [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain"), as well as a 17-acre (6.9 ha) public garden.
* [Children's Museum of Memphis](/wiki/Children%27s_Museum_of_Memphis "Children's Museum of Memphis") – exhibits interactive and educational activities for children.
* [Graceland](/wiki/Graceland "Graceland") – the home of [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), it attracts over 600,000 visitors annually and features two of Presley's airplanes, his automobile and motorcycle collection, and other memorabilia. Graceland is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[146]](#cite_note-nris-149)
* [Pink Palace Museum and Planetarium](/wiki/Pink_Palace_Museum_and_Planetarium "Pink Palace Museum and Planetarium") – a science and historical museum; it includes the third largest [planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium "Planetarium") in the United States and an [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") theater.
* [Beale Street](/wiki/Beale_Street "Beale Street") – a public exhibit honoring Memphis musicians, singers, writers and composers.
* [Mud Island](/wiki/Mud_Island,_Memphis "Mud Island, Memphis") – a park with a walking trail featuring a scale model of the Mississippi River.
* [Mississippi River Museum](/wiki/Mississippi_River_Museum "Mississippi River Museum") – a [maritime museum](/wiki/Maritime_museum "Maritime museum") on Mud Island that focuses on the history of the Mississippi River.
* [Victorian Village](/wiki/Victorian_Village,_Memphis "Victorian Village, Memphis") – a historic district featuring [Victorian-era](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") mansions, some of which are open to the public as museums.
* [The Cotton Museum](/wiki/The_Cotton_Museum "The Cotton Museum") – located on the old trading floor of the [Memphis Cotton Exchange](/wiki/Memphis_Cotton_Exchange "Memphis Cotton Exchange").
* [Metal Museum](/wiki/Metal_Museum "Metal Museum") – features exhibitions of metalwork and public programs featuring metalsmiths.
* [Stax Museum](/wiki/Stax_Museum "Stax Museum") – the former location of [Stax Records](/wiki/Stax_Records "Stax Records").
* [Chucalissa Indian Village](/wiki/Chucalissa_Indian_Village "Chucalissa Indian Village") – a [Walls phase](/wiki/Walls_phase "Walls phase") mound and plaza complex operated by the University of Memphis. The village is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"). The Southeast Indian Heritage Festival is held there annually.[[147]](#cite_note-150)
* [Burkle Estate](/wiki/Burkle_Estate "Burkle Estate") – a historic home now used as a museum of slavery and the anti-slavery movement.[[148]](#cite_note-151)

#### Cemeteries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Cemeteries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Memphis_national_cemetery.jpg/250px-Memphis_national_cemetery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_national_cemetery.jpg)

[Memphis National Cemetery](/wiki/Memphis_National_Cemetery "Memphis National Cemetery")

The [Memphis National Cemetery](/wiki/Memphis_National_Cemetery "Memphis National Cemetery") is a [United States National Cemetery](/wiki/United_States_National_Cemetery "United States National Cemetery") located in northeastern Memphis.

[Historic Elmwood Cemetery](/wiki/Elmwood_Cemetery_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Elmwood Cemetery (Memphis, Tennessee)") is one of the oldest rural garden cemeteries in the South, and contains the [Carlisle S. Page Arboretum](/wiki/Carlisle_S._Page_Arboretum "Carlisle S. Page Arboretum"). [Memorial Park Cemetery](/wiki/Memorial_Park_Cemetery_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Memorial Park Cemetery (Memphis, Tennessee)") is noted for its sculptures by Mexican artist [Dionicio Rodriguez](/wiki/Dionicio_Rodriguez "Dionicio Rodriguez").

Elvis Presley was originally buried in Forest Hill Cemetery, the resting place of his backing band's bassist, [Bill Black](/wiki/Bill_Black "Bill Black"). After an attempted grave robbing, Presley's body was moved and reinterred at the grounds of Graceland.

The [Memphis Grizzlies](/wiki/Memphis_Grizzlies "Memphis Grizzlies") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") is the only team from one of the "[big four](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada")" major sports leagues in Memphis.[[149]](#cite_note-152) The [Memphis Redbirds](/wiki/Memphis_Redbirds "Memphis Redbirds") of the [International League](/wiki/International_League "International League") are a [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") affiliate of the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals").[[150]](#cite_note-BRMemphis-153)

[The University of Memphis](/wiki/Memphis_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "Memphis Tigers men's basketball") [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") team, the [Memphis Tigers](/wiki/Memphis_Tigers "Memphis Tigers"), has a strong following in the city due to a history of competitive success. The Tigers have competed in three NCAA Final Fours (1973, 1985, 2008), with the latter two appearances being vacated. The current coach of the Memphis Tigers is [Penny Hardaway](/wiki/Penny_Hardaway "Penny Hardaway"). Memphis is home to [Simmons Bank Liberty Stadium](/wiki/Simmons_Bank_Liberty_Stadium "Simmons Bank Liberty Stadium"), the site of [University of Memphis](/wiki/University_of_Memphis "University of Memphis") football, the [Liberty Bowl](/wiki/Liberty_Bowl "Liberty Bowl") and the [Southern Heritage Classic](/wiki/Southern_Heritage_Classic "Southern Heritage Classic").

The annual [St. Jude Classic](/wiki/St._Jude_Classic "St. Jude Classic"), a regular part of the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour"), is also held in the city. Each February the city hosts the [Regions Morgan Keegan Championships and the Cellular South Cup](/wiki/Regions_Morgan_Keegan_Championships_and_the_Cellular_South_Cup "Regions Morgan Keegan Championships and the Cellular South Cup"), which are [men's](/wiki/Association_of_Tennis_Professionals "Association of Tennis Professionals") [ATP World Tour 500 series](/wiki/ATP_World_Tour_500_series "ATP World Tour 500 series") and [WTA](/wiki/Women%27s_Tennis_Association "Women's Tennis Association") events, respectively.

Memphis has a significant history in [pro wrestling](/wiki/Pro_wrestling "Pro wrestling"). [Jerry "The King" Lawler](/wiki/Jerry_Lawler "Jerry Lawler") and [Jimmy "The Mouth of the South" Hart](/wiki/Jimmy_Hart "Jimmy Hart") are among the sport's most well-known figures who came out of the city. [Sputnik Monroe](/wiki/Sputnik_Monroe_(wrestler) "Sputnik Monroe (wrestler)"), a wrestler of the 1950s, like Lawler, promoted racial integration in the city. [Ric Flair](/wiki/Ric_Flair "Ric Flair") also noted Memphis as his birthplace.

In the 1970s and early 1980s, the former WFL franchise [Memphis Southmen](/wiki/Memphis_Southmen "Memphis Southmen") / Memphis Grizzlies sued the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") in an attempt to be accepted as an expansion franchise. In 1993, the [Memphis Hound Dogs](/wiki/Memphis_Hound_Dogs "Memphis Hound Dogs") was a proposed NFL expansion that was passed over in favor of the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") and [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers"). The Simmons Bank Liberty Stadium also served as the temporary home of the former [Tennessee Oilers](/wiki/Tennessee_Oilers "Tennessee Oilers") (now the Titans) while the city of [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") worked out stadium issues.

The city is also the site of [Memphis International Raceway](/wiki/Memphis_International_Raceway "Memphis International Raceway"), which held [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") events from 1998 to 2009, when [Dover Motorsports](/wiki/Dover_Motorsports "Dover Motorsports") closed it. In 2011 it reopened under different ownership. It no longer holds NASCAR races, but the [Arca Menards Series](/wiki/ARCA_Menards_Series "ARCA Menards Series") returned to the track in 2020.

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

There are more than 160 parks in Memphis spanning 5,600 acres (2,300 ha).[[151]](#cite_note-154) [Shelby Farms](/wiki/Shelby_Farms "Shelby Farms") park, located at the eastern edge of the city, is one of the largest urban parks in the United States. Other notable Memphis parks include [Tom Lee Park](/wiki/Tom_Lee_Park "Tom Lee Park"); [Overton Park](/wiki/Overton_Park "Overton Park"), including the [Memphis Zoo](/wiki/Memphis_Zoo "Memphis Zoo") and [Old Forest Arboretum](/wiki/Old_Forest_Arboretum_of_Overton_Park "Old Forest Arboretum of Overton Park");[[152]](#cite_note-155) [T. O. Fuller State Park](/wiki/T._O._Fuller_State_Park "T. O. Fuller State Park"); the [Lichterman Nature Center](/wiki/Lichterman_Nature_Center "Lichterman Nature Center"), a nature learning center; the [Memphis Botanic Garden](/wiki/Memphis_Botanic_Garden "Memphis Botanic Garden");[[153]](#cite_note-156) and [Mud Island River Park](/wiki/Mud_Island_River_Park "Mud Island River Park").

The [Wolf River Greenway](/wiki/Wolf_River_Conservancy#Wolf_River_Greenway "Wolf River Conservancy") and [Shelby Farms Greenline](/wiki/Shelby_Farms_Greenline "Shelby Farms Greenline") are two major trails in the Memphis area.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Government_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "Government of Memphis, Tennessee")

See also: [List of mayors of Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Memphis,_Tennessee "List of mayors of Memphis, Tennessee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/City_Hall_from_Front_Street.jpg/250px-City_Hall_from_Front_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Hall_from_Front_Street.jpg)

Memphis City Hall in 2025

Beginning in 1963, Memphis adopted a mayor-council form of government, with 13 [City Council](/wiki/Memphis_City_Council "Memphis City Council") members, six elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") from throughout the city and seven elected from geographic districts. Following passage of the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), civil rights activists challenged the at-large electoral system in court because it made it more difficult for the minority to elect candidates of their choice; at-large voting favored candidates who could command a majority across the city. In 1995, the city adopted a new plan. The 13 Council positions are elected from nine geographic districts: seven are single-member districts and two elect three members each. The mayor is elected in a [non-partisan](/wiki/Non-partisan_democracy "Non-partisan democracy") election.[[154]](#cite_note-:0-157) The current mayor, [Paul Young](/wiki/Paul_Young_(Tennessee_politician) "Paul Young (Tennessee politician)"), is a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") and took office on January 1, 2024.[[154]](#cite_note-:0-157)

Since the late 20th century, regional discussions have recurred on the concept of consolidating unincorporated [Shelby County](/wiki/Shelby_County,_Tennessee "Shelby County, Tennessee") and Memphis into a [metropolitan government](/wiki/Metropolitan_government "Metropolitan government"), as [Nashville-Davidson County](/wiki/Nashville-Davidson_County "Nashville-Davidson County") did in 1963. Consolidation was a referendum item on the 2010 ballots in both the city of Memphis and Shelby County, under the state law for dual-voting on such measures. The referendum was controversial in both jurisdictions.[[155]](#cite_note-consvote-158) In October 2010 before the vote, eight Shelby County citizens had filed a lawsuit against the dual-voting requirement, arguing that total votes for the referendum should be counted together. City voters narrowly supported the measure for consolidation with 50.8% in favor; county voters overwhelmingly voted against the measure with 85% against. In total voting, 64% of voters opposed the consolidation.[[156]](#cite_note-dismiss-159) In 2014, the federal district court dismissed the lawsuit, on the grounds that the referendum would have failed when both jurisdictions' votes were counted together. The [U.S. Sixth District Court of Appeals](/wiki/U.S._Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Sixth_Circuit "U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit") upheld that decision.[[156]](#cite_note-dismiss-159)

In the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), Memphis is split between two congressional districts. Most of the city, including [downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Memphis,_Tennessee "Downtown Memphis, Tennessee"), is located in the [9th District](/wiki/Tennessee%27s_9th_congressional_district "Tennessee's 9th congressional district"), represented by Democrat [Steve Cohen](/wiki/Steve_Cohen_(politician) "Steve Cohen (politician)"). The eastern fourth of the city is in the [8th District](/wiki/Tennessee%27s_8th_congressional_district "Tennessee's 8th congressional district"), represented by Republican [David Kustoff](/wiki/David_Kustoff "David Kustoff") of nearby [Germantown](/wiki/Germantown,_Tennessee "Germantown, Tennessee").[[157]](#cite_note-160)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Administration_Building_main_entrance_UofM.jpg/250px-Administration_Building_main_entrance_UofM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Administration_Building_main_entrance_UofM.jpg)

The [University of Memphis](/wiki/University_of_Memphis "University of Memphis")

### Primary and secondary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Primary and secondary")]

The city is served by [Memphis-Shelby County Schools](/wiki/Shelby_County_Schools_(Tennessee) "Shelby County Schools (Tennessee)") (formerly Shelby County Schools). On March 8, 2011, residents voted to dissolve the charter for [Memphis City Schools](/wiki/Memphis_City_Schools "Memphis City Schools"), effectively merging it with the Shelby County School District.[[158]](#cite_note-161) After issues with state law and court challenges, the merger took effect the start of the 2013–14 school year. In Shelby County, six incorporated cities voted to establish separate school systems in 2013.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Memphis-Shelby County School System operates 222 elementary, middle, and high schools.[[159]](#cite_note-MSCSData-162)

The Memphis area is also home to many private, college-prep schools, including:

* [Briarcrest Christian School](/wiki/Briarcrest_Christian_School "Briarcrest Christian School") (co-ed)
* [Christian Brothers High School](/wiki/Christian_Brothers_High_School_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Christian Brothers High School (Memphis, Tennessee)") (boys)
* [Evangelical Christian School](/wiki/Evangelical_Christian_School "Evangelical Christian School") (co-ed)
* [First Assembly Christian School](/wiki/First_Assembly_Christian_School "First Assembly Christian School") (co-ed)
* [St. Mary's Episcopal School](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Episcopal_School "St. Mary's Episcopal School") (girls)
* [Hutchison School](/wiki/Hutchison_School "Hutchison School") (girls)
* [Lausanne Collegiate School](/wiki/Lausanne_Collegiate_School "Lausanne Collegiate School") (co-ed)
* [Memphis University School](/wiki/Memphis_University_School "Memphis University School")[[d]](#cite_note-163) (boys)
* [Saint Benedict at Auburndale](/wiki/Saint_Benedict_at_Auburndale "Saint Benedict at Auburndale") (co-ed)
* [St. Agnes Academy](/wiki/St._Agnes_Academy-St._Dominic_School "St. Agnes Academy-St. Dominic School") (girls)
* [Immaculate Conception Cathedral School](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception_Cathedral_School_(Memphis,_Tennessee) "Immaculate Conception Cathedral School (Memphis, Tennessee)") (girls)
* Elliston Baptist Academy (co-ed)
* [Memphis Harding Academy](/wiki/Memphis_Harding_Academy "Memphis Harding Academy") (co-ed)

### Postsecondary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Postsecondary")]

Colleges and universities in the city include:

* The [University of Memphis](/wiki/University_of_Memphis "University of Memphis")
* [Rhodes College](/wiki/Rhodes_College "Rhodes College")
* [Christian Brothers University](/wiki/Christian_Brothers_University "Christian Brothers University")
* [LeMoyne–Owen College](/wiki/LeMoyne%E2%80%93Owen_College "LeMoyne–Owen College")
* [Baptist Health Sciences University](/wiki/Baptist_Health_Sciences_University "Baptist Health Sciences University")
* [Memphis Theological Seminary](/wiki/Memphis_Theological_Seminary "Memphis Theological Seminary")
* [Harding School of Theology](/wiki/Harding_School_of_Theology "Harding School of Theology")
* [Reformed Theological Seminary](/wiki/Reformed_Theological_Seminary "Reformed Theological Seminary") (satellite campus)
* William R. Moore College of Technology
* [Southern College of Optometry](/wiki/Southern_College_of_Optometry "Southern College of Optometry")
* [Southwest Tennessee Community College](/wiki/Southwest_Tennessee_Community_College "Southwest Tennessee Community College")
* Tennessee Technology Center at Memphis
* Visible Music College
* [Mid-America Baptist Theological Seminary](/wiki/Mid-America_Baptist_Theological_Seminary "Mid-America Baptist Theological Seminary")
* The [University of Tennessee Health Science Center](/wiki/University_of_Tennessee_Health_Science_Center "University of Tennessee Health Science Center").

Memphis also has campuses of several for-profit post-secondary institutions, including Concorde Career College, [ITT Technical Institute](/wiki/ITT_Technical_Institute "ITT Technical Institute"), [Vatterott College](/wiki/Vatterott_College "Vatterott College"),[[160]](#cite_note-164) and [University of Phoenix](/wiki/University_of_Phoenix "University of Phoenix"). [Remington College](/wiki/Remington_College "Remington College")[[161]](#cite_note-165) is a local nonprofit post-secondary institution.

The [University of Tennessee College of Dentistry](/wiki/University_of_Tennessee_College_of_Dentistry "University of Tennessee College of Dentistry") was founded in 1878, making it the oldest dental college in the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), and the third oldest public college of [dentistry](/wiki/Dentistry "Dentistry") in the United States.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

### Cultural references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Cultural references")]

#### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Music")]

Memphis is the subject of numerous pop and country songs, including "[The Memphis Blues](/wiki/The_Memphis_Blues "The Memphis Blues")" by [W. C. Handy](/wiki/W._C._Handy "W. C. Handy"), "[Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee_(song) "Memphis, Tennessee (song)")" by [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry"), "Night Train to Memphis" by [Roy Acuff](/wiki/Roy_Acuff "Roy Acuff"), "Goin' to Memphis" by [Paul Revere and the Raiders](/wiki/Paul_Revere_and_the_Raiders "Paul Revere and the Raiders"), "[Queen of Memphis](/wiki/Queen_of_Memphis "Queen of Memphis")" by [Confederate Railroad](/wiki/Confederate_Railroad "Confederate Railroad"), "[Memphis Soul Stew](/wiki/Memphis_Soul_Stew "Memphis Soul Stew")" by [King Curtis](/wiki/King_Curtis "King Curtis"), "[Maybe It Was Memphis](/wiki/Maybe_It_Was_Memphis "Maybe It Was Memphis")" by [Pam Tillis](/wiki/Pam_Tillis "Pam Tillis"), "[Graceland](/wiki/Graceland_(song) "Graceland (song)")" by [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon"), "Memphis Train" by [Rufus Thomas](/wiki/Rufus_Thomas "Rufus Thomas"), "[All the Way from Memphis](/wiki/All_the_Way_from_Memphis "All the Way from Memphis")" by [Mott the Hoople](/wiki/Mott_the_Hoople "Mott the Hoople"), "[Wrong Side of Memphis](/wiki/Wrong_Side_of_Memphis "Wrong Side of Memphis")" by [Trisha Yearwood](/wiki/Trisha_Yearwood "Trisha Yearwood"), "[Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again](/wiki/Stuck_Inside_of_Mobile_with_the_Memphis_Blues_Again "Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again")" by [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), "Memphis Skyline" by [Rufus Wainwright](/wiki/Rufus_Wainwright "Rufus Wainwright"), "Sequestered in Memphis" by [the Hold Steady](/wiki/The_Hold_Steady "The Hold Steady") and "[Walking in Memphis](/wiki/Walking_in_Memphis "Walking in Memphis")" by [Marc Cohn](/wiki/Marc_Cohn "Marc Cohn").

In addition, Memphis is mentioned in scores of other songs, including "[Proud Mary](/wiki/Proud_Mary "Proud Mary")" by [Creedence Clearwater Revival](/wiki/Creedence_Clearwater_Revival "Creedence Clearwater Revival"), "[Honky Tonk Women](/wiki/Honky_Tonk_Women "Honky Tonk Women")" by [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), "[Dixie Chicken](/wiki/Dixie_Chicken_(album) "Dixie Chicken (album)")" by [Little Feat](/wiki/Little_Feat "Little Feat"), "[Who's Gonna Fill Their Shoes](/wiki/Who%27s_Gonna_Fill_Their_Shoes "Who's Gonna Fill Their Shoes")" by [George Jones](/wiki/George_Jones "George Jones"), "Daisy Jane" by [America](/wiki/America_(band) "America (band)"), "[Life Is a Highway](/wiki/Life_Is_a_Highway "Life Is a Highway")" by [Tom Cochrane](/wiki/Tom_Cochrane "Tom Cochrane"), "[Black Velvet](/wiki/Black_Velvet_(song) "Black Velvet (song)")" by [Alannah Myles](/wiki/Alannah_Myles "Alannah Myles"), "[Cities](/wiki/Cities_(song) "Cities (song)")" by [Talking Heads](/wiki/Talking_Heads "Talking Heads"), "Crazed Country Rebel" by [Hank Williams III](/wiki/Hank_Williams_III "Hank Williams III"), "[Pride (In the Name of Love)](/wiki/Pride_(In_the_Name_of_Love) "Pride (In the Name of Love)")" by [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2"), "M.E.M.P.H.I.S." by the [Disco Biscuits](/wiki/Disco_Biscuits "Disco Biscuits"), "New New Minglewood Blues" and "Candyman" by [the Grateful Dead](/wiki/The_Grateful_Dead "The Grateful Dead"), "You Should Be Glad" by [Widespread Panic](/wiki/Widespread_Panic "Widespread Panic"), "Roll With Me" by [8Ball & MJG](/wiki/8Ball_%26_MJG "8Ball & MJG"), "Someday" by [Steve Earle](/wiki/Steve_Earle "Steve Earle") and popularly recorded by [Shawn Colvin](/wiki/Shawn_Colvin "Shawn Colvin"), and many others.

More than 1,000 commercial recordings of over 800 distinct songs contain "Memphis" in them. The [Memphis Rock N' Soul Museum](/wiki/Memphis_Rock_N%27_Soul_Museum "Memphis Rock N' Soul Museum") maintains an ever-updated list of these on its website.[[172]](#cite_note-176)
