#### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Literature")]

Many works of fiction and literature are set in Memphis. These include *[The Reivers](/wiki/The_Reivers "The Reivers")* by [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner") (1962), *September, September* by Shelby Foote (1977); [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Matthew_Hillsman_Taylor "Peter Matthew Hillsman Taylor")'s *The Old Forest and Other Stories* (1985), and his [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize")-winning *[A Summons to Memphis](/wiki/A_Summons_to_Memphis "A Summons to Memphis")* (1986); *[The Firm](/wiki/The_Firm_(novel) "The Firm (novel)")* (1991), *[The Client](/wiki/The_Client_(novel) "The Client (novel)")* (1993) and [The Rainmaker](/wiki/The_Rainmaker_(novel) "The Rainmaker (novel)"), all by [John Grisham](/wiki/John_Grisham "John Grisham"); *Memphis Afternoons: a Memoir* by James Conaway (1993), *Plague of Dreamers* by Steve Stern (1997); *[Cassina Gambrel Was Missing](/wiki/Cassina_Gambrel_Was_Missing "Cassina Gambrel Was Missing")* by William Watkins (1999); *The Guardian* by Beecher Smith (1999), "We are Billion-Year-Old Carbon" by Corey Mesler (2005), *[The Silence of the Lambs](/wiki/The_Silence_of_the_Lambs_(novel) "The Silence of the Lambs (novel)")* by [Thomas Harris](/wiki/Thomas_Harris "Thomas Harris"), and *The Architect* by James Williamson (2007).

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Transportation_in_Memphis,_Tennessee "Transportation in Memphis, Tennessee")

#### Highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Highways")]

[I-40](/wiki/Interstate_40_in_Tennessee "Interstate 40 in Tennessee"), [I-55](/wiki/Interstate_55_in_Tennessee "Interstate 55 in Tennessee"), [I-22](/wiki/Interstate_22 "Interstate 22"), [I-240](/wiki/Interstate_240_(Tennessee) "Interstate 240 (Tennessee)"), [I-269](/wiki/Interstate_269 "Interstate 269"), and [SR 385](/wiki/Tennessee_State_Route_385 "Tennessee State Route 385") are the main expressways in the Memphis area. I-40 and I-55 cross the Mississippi River into Memphis from the state of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas").[[174]](#cite_note-tdotmap-178) [Interstate 69](/wiki/Interstate_69_in_Tennessee "Interstate 69 in Tennessee") is a proposed interstate that, upon completion, would connect Memphis to Canada and Mexico.[[175]](#cite_note-tdottimeline-179)

I-40 is a coast-to-coast freeway that connects Memphis to [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") and on to [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") to the east, and [Little Rock](/wiki/Little_Rock,_Arkansas "Little Rock, Arkansas"), [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City"), and the [Los Angeles area](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles") to the west. I-55 connects Memphis to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") and Chicago to the north, and [Jackson](/wiki/Jackson,_Mississippi "Jackson, Mississippi") and [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") to the south. I-240 is the inner beltway which serves areas including Downtown, Midtown, South Memphis, [Memphis International Airport](/wiki/Memphis_International_Airport "Memphis International Airport"), East Memphis, and North Memphis.[[174]](#cite_note-tdotmap-178) I-269 is the larger, outer interstate loop immediately serving the suburbs of [Millington](/wiki/Millington,_Tennessee "Millington, Tennessee"), Eads, [Arlington](/wiki/Arlington,_Tennessee "Arlington, Tennessee"), [Collierville](/wiki/Collierville,_Tennessee "Collierville, Tennessee"), and [Hernando, Mississippi](/wiki/Hernando,_Mississippi "Hernando, Mississippi"). It was completed in 2018.[[176]](#cite_note-180)

I-22 connects Memphis with [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), via northern Mississippi (including [Tupelo](/wiki/Tupelo,_Mississippi "Tupelo, Mississippi")) and northwestern Alabama. While technically not entering the city of Memphis proper, I-22 ends at I-269 in [Byhalia](/wiki/Byhalia,_Mississippi "Byhalia, Mississippi"), connecting it to the rest of the Memphis interstate system.

I-69 is proposed to follow I-55 and I-240 through the city of Memphis. Once completed, I-69 will link Memphis with [Port Huron](/wiki/Port_Huron,_Michigan "Port Huron, Michigan") via [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"), and [Brownsville](/wiki/Brownsville,_Texas "Brownsville, Texas") via [Shreveport](/wiki/Shreveport,_Louisiana "Shreveport, Louisiana") and [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), Texas.[[175]](#cite_note-tdottimeline-179)

A new spur, [I-555](/wiki/Interstate_555 "Interstate 555"), also serves the Memphis metro area connecting it to [Jonesboro](/wiki/Jonesboro,_Arkansas "Jonesboro, Arkansas").

Other important federal highways though Memphis include the east–west [US 70](/wiki/U.S._Route_70_in_Tennessee "U.S. Route 70 in Tennessee"), [US 64](/wiki/U.S._Route_64_in_Tennessee "U.S. Route 64 in Tennessee"), and [US 72](/wiki/U.S._Route_72#West_Tennessee "U.S. Route 72"); and the north–south [US 51](/wiki/U.S._Route_51_in_Tennessee "U.S. Route 51 in Tennessee") and [US 61](/wiki/U.S._Route_61#Tennessee "U.S. Route 61").[[174]](#cite_note-tdotmap-178) The former is the historic highway north to Chicago via [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo,_Illinois "Cairo, Illinois"), while the latter roughly parallels the Mississippi River for most of its course and crosses the [Mississippi Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_Delta "Mississippi Delta") region to the south, with the Delta also legendary for Blues music.

**Roadways**

Memphis maintains 6,800 lane-miles of city roadways. The city collaborated with [Google Cloud Platform](/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform "Google Cloud Platform") and SpringML in February 2019 to test [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") (ML) to improve public services. A key focus is pothole identification using [TensorFlow](/wiki/TensorFlow "TensorFlow") technology.[[177]](#cite_note-181) Public Works personnel completed 63,000 repairs, with around 7,500 of those reported by citizens to 311.[[178]](#cite_note-182)

#### Transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Transit")]

The [Memphis Area Transit Authority](/wiki/Memphis_Area_Transit_Authority "Memphis Area Transit Authority") provides local transit services around Memphis, including the [MATA Trolley](/wiki/MATA_Trolley "MATA Trolley") heritage streetcar system. Intercity bus service to the city is provided by [Flixbus](/wiki/Flixbus "Flixbus"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), and [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines").[[179]](#cite_note-183)[[180]](#cite_note-184)

#### Railroads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Railroads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Memphis_Arkansas_Bridge_Memphis_TN_2012-07-22_016.jpg/250px-Memphis_Arkansas_Bridge_Memphis_TN_2012-07-22_016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memphis_Arkansas_Bridge_Memphis_TN_2012-07-22_016.jpg)

Three bridges over the Mississippi

A large volume of railroad freight moves through Memphis, because of its two heavy-duty Mississippi River railroad crossings, which carry several major east–west railroad freight lines, and also because of the major north–south railroad lines through Memphis which connect with such major cities as Chicago, [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"), [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), [Mobile](/wiki/Mobile,_Alabama "Mobile, Alabama"), and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama").

By the early 20th century, Memphis had two major passenger railroad stations, which made the city a regional hub for trains coming from the north, east, south and west. After passenger railroad service declined heavily through the middle of the 20th century, the [Memphis Union Station](/wiki/Memphis_Union_Station "Memphis Union Station") was demolished in 1969. The [Memphis Central Station](/wiki/Central_Station_(Memphis) "Central Station (Memphis)")[[181]](#cite_note-185) was eventually renovated, and it still serves the city. The only inter-city passenger railroad service to Memphis is the daily *[City of New Orleans](/wiki/City_of_New_Orleans_(train) "City of New Orleans (train)")* train, operated by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), which has one train northbound and one train southbound each day between Chicago and New Orleans.

##### Railroads, common freight carriers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Railroads, common freight carriers")]

* [BNSF Railway](/wiki/BNSF_Railway "BNSF Railway") (BNSF)
* [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway") (CN) through subsidiary [Illinois Central Railroad](/wiki/Illinois_Central_Railroad "Illinois Central Railroad") (IC)
* [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") (CSXT)
* [Canadian Pacific Kansas City](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Kansas_City "Canadian Pacific Kansas City") (CPKC)
* [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway") (NS), including subsidiaries [Alabama Great Southern Railroad](/wiki/Alabama_Great_Southern_Railroad "Alabama Great Southern Railroad") (AGS), [Central of Georgia Railroad](/wiki/Central_of_Georgia_Railroad "Central of Georgia Railroad") (CG), [Cincinnati, New Orleans and Texas Pacific Railway](/wiki/Cincinnati,_New_Orleans_and_Texas_Pacific_Railway "Cincinnati, New Orleans and Texas Pacific Railway") (CNTP), [Tennessee Railway](/wiki/Tennessee_Railway "Tennessee Railway") (TENN), and [Tennessee, Alabama and Georgia Railway](/wiki/Tennessee,_Alabama_and_Georgia_Railway "Tennessee, Alabama and Georgia Railway") (TAG)
* [R.J. Corman Railroad/Memphis Line](/wiki/R.J._Corman_Railroad/Memphis_Line "R.J. Corman Railroad/Memphis Line") (RJCM)
* [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") (UP)

##### Railroads, passenger carriers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Railroads, passenger carriers")]

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") (AMTK)

#### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Airports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/FedEx_plane_Memphis_TN_001.jpg/250px-FedEx_plane_Memphis_TN_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:FedEx_plane_Memphis_TN_001.jpg)

FedEx aircraft at Memphis International Airport

[Memphis International Airport](/wiki/Memphis_International_Airport "Memphis International Airport") is the global "SuperHub" of [FedEx Express](/wiki/FedEx_Express "FedEx Express"), and has the largest cargo operations by volume of any airport worldwide, surpassing [Hong Kong International Airport](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport") in 2021.[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187)

Memphis International ranks as the 41st busiest passenger airport in the US and served as a hub for [Northwest Airlines](/wiki/Northwest_Airlines "Northwest Airlines") (later [Delta Air Lines](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines")) until September 3, 2013.[[184]](#cite_note-188) It had 4.39 million boarding passengers (enplanements) in 2011, an 11.9% decrease over the previous year.[[185]](#cite_note-189) Delta has reduced its flights at Memphis by approximately 65% since its 2008 merger with Northwest Airlines and operates an average of 30 daily flights as of December 2013, with two international destinations (Cancún – seasonally; Toronto year-round). Delta Air Lines announced the closing of its Memphis pilot and crew base in 2012. Other airlines providing passenger service are: [Southwest Airlines](/wiki/Southwest_Airlines "Southwest Airlines"); [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines"); [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines"); [Allegiant](/wiki/Allegiant_Air "Allegiant Air"); [Frontier](/wiki/Frontier_Airlines "Frontier Airlines"); [Air Canada](/wiki/Air_Canada "Air Canada"); and Southern Vacations Express.[[186]](#cite_note-190)

There are also [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airports in the Memphis Metropolitan Area, including the [Millington Regional Jetport](/wiki/Millington_Regional_Jetport "Millington Regional Jetport"), located at the former Naval Air Station in [Millington, Tennessee](/wiki/Millington,_Tennessee "Millington, Tennessee").

#### River port

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: River port")]

Main article: [Port of Memphis](/wiki/Port_of_Memphis "Port of Memphis")

Memphis has the second-busiest cargo port on the Mississippi River, which is also the fourth-busiest inland port in the United States.[[187]](#cite_note-191) The International Port of Memphis covers both the Tennessee and Arkansas sides of the Mississippi River from [river mile](/wiki/River_mile "River mile") 725 (km 1167) to mile 740 (km 1191).[[188]](#cite_note-192) A focal point of the river port is the [industrial park](/wiki/Industrial_park "Industrial park") on [President's Island](/wiki/President%27s_Island "President's Island"), just south of [Downtown Memphis](/wiki/Downtown_Memphis,_Tennessee "Downtown Memphis, Tennessee").

#### Bridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Bridges")]

Four railroad and highway bridges cross the Mississippi River at Memphis. In order of their opening years, these are the [Frisco Bridge](/wiki/Frisco_Bridge "Frisco Bridge") (1892, [single-track](/wiki/Single_track_(rail) "Single track (rail)") rail), the [Harahan Bridge](/wiki/Harahan_Bridge "Harahan Bridge") (1916, a [road-rail bridge](/wiki/Road-rail_bridge "Road-rail bridge") until 1949, currently carries [double-track](/wiki/Double-track "Double-track") rail), the [Memphis-Arkansas Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Memphis-Arkansas_Memorial_Bridge "Memphis-Arkansas Memorial Bridge") (Highway, 1949; later incorporated into [I-55](/wiki/Interstate_55 "Interstate 55")), and the [Hernando de Soto Bridge](/wiki/Hernando_de_Soto_Bridge "Hernando de Soto Bridge") ([I-40](/wiki/Interstate_40 "Interstate 40"), 1973). A bicycle/pedestrian walkway opened along the Harahan Bridge in late 2016, utilizing the former westbound roadway.[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[190]](#cite_note-194)[[191]](#cite_note-195)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Utilities")]

Memphis's primary utility provider is the [Memphis Light, Gas and Water](/wiki/Memphis_Light,_Gas_and_Water "Memphis Light, Gas and Water") Division (MLGW). This is the largest three-service municipal utility in the United States, providing electricity, natural gas, and pure water service to all residents of Shelby County. Prior to that, Memphis was served by two primary electric companies, which were merged into the Memphis Power Company.[[192]](#cite_note-MLGWhistory-196)

The City of Memphis bought the private company in 1939 to form MLGW,[[192]](#cite_note-MLGWhistory-196)[[193]](#cite_note-197) which was an early customer of electricity from the [Tennessee Valley Authority](/wiki/Tennessee_Valley_Authority "Tennessee Valley Authority") (TVA). In 1954 the [Dixon-Yates contract](/wiki/Dixon-Yates_contract "Dixon-Yates contract") was proposed to make more power available to the city from the TVA, but the contract was cancelled; it had been an issue for the Democrats in the [1954 Congressional elections](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections,_1954 "United States House of Representatives elections, 1954").

MLGW still buys most of its power from TVA, and the company pumps its own fresh water from the Memphis Aquifer, using more than 180 water wells.

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Memphis,_Tennessee&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Health care")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/St_Judes_grass.jpg/250px-St_Judes_grass.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Judes_grass.jpg)

[St. Jude Children's Research Hospital](/wiki/St._Jude_Children%27s_Research_Hospital "St. Jude Children's Research Hospital")

The Memphis and Shelby County region supports numerous hospitals, including the Methodist and Baptist Memorial health systems, two of the nation's largest private hospitals. Until the 1960s and the end of [segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation_in_the_United_States "Racial segregation in the United States"), most hospitals only served white patients. One of the few hospitals for African Americans in Memphis in those times was Collins Chapel Connectional Hospital, whose historic building now houses a homeless shelter.[[194]](#cite_note-198)

[Methodist Le Bonheur Healthcare](/wiki/Methodist_Le_Bonheur_Healthcare "Methodist Le Bonheur Healthcare"), the largest healthcare provider in the Memphis region and the fourth largest employer as of 2018,[[195]](#cite_note-199) operates seven hospitals and several rural clinics. Methodist Healthcare operates, among others, the [Le Bonheur Children's Hospital](/wiki/Le_Bonheur_Children%27s_Hospital "Le Bonheur Children's Hospital"), which offers primary level 1 pediatric trauma care, as well as a nationally recognized pediatric brain tumor program. Methodist Healthcare also operates [Methodist University Hospital](/wiki/Methodist_University_Hospital "Methodist University Hospital"), a 617-bed facility 1 mile southeast of Le Bonheur.

Baptist Memorial Healthcare operates fifteen hospitals (three in Memphis), including [Baptist Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Baptist_Memorial_Hospital-Memphis "Baptist Memorial Hospital-Memphis"), and with a merger in 2018 became the largest healthcare system in the mid-South.[[196]](#cite_note-200) According to Health Care Market Guide's annual studies, Mid-Southerners have named Baptist Memorial their "preferred hospital choice for quality".

The [St. Jude Children's Research Hospital](/wiki/St._Jude_Children%27s_Research_Hospital "St. Jude Children's Research Hospital"), leading pediatric treatment and research facility focused on children's catastrophic diseases, resides in Memphis. The institution was conceived and built by entertainer [Danny Thomas](/wiki/Danny_Thomas "Danny Thomas") in 1962 as a tribute to [St. Jude Thaddeus](/wiki/Jude_the_Apostle "Jude the Apostle"), patron saint of impossible, hopeless, and difficult causes.

Regional One Health is located in Memphis.[[197]](#cite_note-201)

Memphis is home to Delta Medical Center of Memphis,[[198]](#cite_note-202) which is the only employee-owned medical facility in North America.

Individual [health insurance marketplace](/wiki/Health_insurance_marketplace "Health insurance marketplace") insurers are limited, with [Bright Health](/wiki/Bright_Health "Bright Health") and [Cigna](/wiki/Cigna "Cigna") offering coverage in the area.[[199]](#cite_note-203)
